class_schedule.topic: Entrepreneurship as Discipline: Myths, Mindsets, and Types of Ventures
learning_objectives: Distinguish entrepreneurship from general management and small business ownership using Shane and Venkataraman's opportunity-based definition
Debunk common myths about entrepreneurs (born not made, risk-lovers, lone genius, young only) using empirical research from Kauffman Foundation and MIT
Classify venture types (lifestyle, small business, scalable startup, social, corporate intrapreneurship) and match each to appropriate funding, growth, and exit strategies
Evaluate the entrepreneurial mindset components: tolerance for ambiguity, bias toward action, resourcefulness, and evidence-based iteration
readings: Ries, The Lean Startup (2011), Introduction and Ch. 1 - Start
Thiel & Masters, Zero to One (2014), Ch. 1-2 - The Challenge of the Future and Party Like It's 1999
Shane & Venkataraman, 'The Promise of Entrepreneurship as a Field of Research', Academy of Management Review (2000)
Kauffman Foundation, 'The Anatomy of an Entrepreneur' (2009) - empirical study of 549 U.S. startup founders

lecture_notes.introduction: Entrepreneurship has a branding problem. Popular culture paints entrepreneurs as 22-year-old Stanford dropouts in hoodies who had a lightning-bolt idea in a garage and became billionaires by 25. The reality, documented by the Kauffman Foundation's study of 549 American startup founders, is almost the opposite: the average successful founder was 40 years old at the time of founding, had 16 years of industry experience, was married with children, came from a middle-class background, and had previously worked at an established company. Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia of Airbnb are famous for maxing out credit cards and selling Obama O's cereal boxes at the 2008 Democratic National Convention to keep their company alive after being rejected by every major VC they approached. That is not a lightning bolt; that is grinding through rejection with a better-than-everyone understanding of a real customer problem. This course rejects the mythology and treats entrepreneurship as a learnable discipline grounded in Blank's customer development methodology, Ries's Lean Startup approach, and Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas. You will learn to identify genuine opportunities, test hypotheses cheaply before betting capital, build minimum viable products, raise capital when appropriate, and understand why most startups fail and how to avoid the predictable traps.
key_concepts: Shane and Venkataraman definition: entrepreneurship is the scholarly examination of how, by whom, and with what effects opportunities to create future goods and services are discovered, evaluated, and exploited; this opportunity-centric framing separates entrepreneurship from general management

Opportunity vs idea: an idea is a concept, an opportunity is an idea plus a real market, a viable business model, and a feasible path to execution; most 'startup ideas' are not opportunities because no evidence supports demand or willingness to pay

Venture typology: lifestyle businesses (consulting firm, restaurant) optimize owner quality of life; small businesses (dry cleaner, franchise) replicate proven models; scalable startups (SaaS, marketplaces) pursue exponential growth with VC funding; social ventures pursue mission plus financial sustainability; intrapreneurship drives innovation inside existing corporations

Myth 1 - born not made: longitudinal studies show entrepreneurial success correlates with deliberate practice, domain expertise, network breadth, and prior operating experience, not innate traits; the Collison brothers had built and sold Auctomatic for $5M at ages 17 and 19 before Stripe

Myth 2 - entrepreneurs are risk-lovers: research by Sarasvathy and others shows successful entrepreneurs are actually risk managers who use effectuation (control what you can, affordable loss, leverage contingencies) rather than gambling; they test cheaply before betting significantly

Myth 3 - young only: Azoulay et al. (NBER, 2018) found the average age of successful startup founders is 42; founders aged 50 are twice as likely to build top-performing startups as founders aged 30; experience compounds

Myth 4 - lone genius: virtually every major tech company had co-founders (Gates and Allen, Jobs and Wozniak, Page and Brin, Chesky with Gebbia and Blecharczyk); Noam Wasserman's The Founder's Dilemmas documents that solo founders have substantially lower success rates

Entrepreneurial mindset: tolerance for ambiguity, bias toward action, resourcefulness (doing more with less), intellectual honesty about what is working, and speed of learning from each experiment; Ries calls the fundamental metric 'validated learning per dollar spent'

Effectuation vs causation (Sarasvathy): expert entrepreneurs use effectuation - start with means at hand, focus on affordable loss, leverage contingencies and partnerships; this contrasts with the MBA causation model of market research then planning then execution

Why this matters to non-founders: even students who never start a company will work with startups, invest in them, sell to them, compete against them, or drive innovation inside established firms; these frameworks translate directly to corporate strategy, VC, and product management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Airbnb origin story as a living case study against the myth-busting framework. Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia were 27-year-old industrial designers, not Stanford computer scientists; they were broke, not rich, when they started. They had an immediate problem (rent due, design conference in San Francisco sold out hotels), turned it into a hypothesis (people might pay to sleep on an air mattress in a stranger's apartment), built the crudest possible MVP (a WordPress site called airbedandbreakfast.com with photos of their own apartment), and tested with three real customers that first weekend. They pivoted repeatedly, were rejected by seven prominent Silicon Valley investors, and kept the lights on by designing and selling limited-edition cereal boxes (Obama O's and Cap'n McCain's) during the 2008 election. Paul Graham eventually accepted them into Y Combinator not because of the idea but because of the founders' resourcefulness. The lesson: Airbnb succeeded because of domain insight, tight feedback loops with real customers, and willingness to look ridiculous.
setup_requirements: Laptop with access to Crunchbase free tier for looking up funding histories
Copy of The Lean Startup and Zero to One (print or ebook)
Notebook or digital doc for personal opportunity journal assignment (weekly)
Access to YouTube for Y Combinator Startup School videos and founder interviews

review.value: The unit of analysis in entrepreneurship is the opportunity (a real market with real willingness to pay and a feasible path to serve it), not the idea. Ideas are cheap and nearly worthless; opportunities are rare and are what sophisticated founders and investors evaluate

A scalable startup pursuing VC funding is a completely different game from a lifestyle business optimizing owner freedom. Choosing the wrong venture type for your actual goals is one of the most destructive errors a founder can make; many founders take VC money and regret it when growth requirements destroy the lifestyle they wanted

The empirical data (Azoulay et al. 2018, Kauffman 2009) shows experienced founders dramatically outperform first-time young founders. Domain expertise, network, and pattern recognition from prior roles are not handicaps to entrepreneurship; they are the foundation for identifying high-quality opportunities

Successful entrepreneurs manage risk through cheap experiments, affordable loss thinking, and validated learning. The stereotype of the risk-loving gambler is wrong; the best founders are obsessed with reducing uncertainty as cheaply and quickly as possible

learning_objectives: Apply systematic opportunity recognition frameworks (trend analysis, pain point discovery, adjacent possible, first-principles) to generate venture opportunities
Evaluate opportunities using a structured scorecard including market size, founder-market fit, willingness to pay, and defensibility
Distinguish vitamins (nice-to-have) from painkillers (must-have) and understand why painkillers are typically better venture foundations
Recognize cognitive biases (confirmation, survivorship, availability) that distort opportunity evaluation and apply debiasing techniques
readings: Blank & Dorf, The Startup Owner's Manual (2012), Ch. 1 - The Path
Christensen, The Innovator's Dilemma (1997), Ch. 1-2 - How Can Great Firms Fail
Thiel & Masters, Zero to One (2014), Ch. 3-4 - All Happy Companies Are Different; The Ideology of Competition
Graham, 'How to Get Startup Ideas' (paulgraham.com essay, 2012)

lecture_notes.introduction: Where do good startup opportunities come from? The romantic answer is 'a flash of inspiration in the shower,' but the empirical answer is far more systematic. Paul Graham's essay How to Get Startup Ideas makes the now-famous argument that the best startup ideas come not from trying to think of startup ideas, but from noticing problems while immersed in some domain. Drew Houston, founder of Dropbox, built the company because he kept forgetting his USB drive on trips; he lived the problem before he solved it. The Collison brothers built Stripe because as developers they experienced the genuine pain of accepting credit cards online, with PayPal and Authorize.net forcing days of integration work on every new project. Brian Armstrong started Coinbase because he wanted to use Bitcoin and found the existing exchanges unusable and untrustworthy. The pattern is consistent: live the problem, understand it more deeply than anyone, then build something better. This session teaches you to systematically hunt for opportunities, evaluate them using a rigorous scorecard, and recognize the cognitive traps that cause most founders to fall in love with ideas that cannot become real businesses. Quibi spent 1.75 billion dollars on premium short-form mobile video that no one wanted; the technology was impressive, the content was expensive, and the opportunity was imaginary.
key_concepts: Trend analysis: look for shifts (technology, regulation, demographics, behavior) that create windows where old solutions are obsolete and new ones are possible; Uber and Airbnb both emerged from the smartphone and GPS revolution around 2008-2010

Pain point discovery: interview 20+ people in a domain, ask about frustrations, workarounds, and things they have paid to fix; Eric Ries recommends spending more time understanding the problem than designing the solution

Adjacent possible (Stuart Kauffman concept adapted): the next venture opportunities often exist just beyond current capabilities or business models; YouTube was not possible until broadband and Flash video, Instagram was not possible until smartphone cameras

First-principles thinking (Thiel, Musk): question every assumption of the current industry and rebuild from physics/economics; SpaceX questioned why rockets had to cost billions and built reusable ones; Tesla questioned why electric cars had to be slow and ugly

Painkillers vs vitamins: painkillers solve urgent problems people pay to eliminate (tax software, payroll, payments); vitamins offer nice-to-haves people might pay for; painkillers typically make better venture foundations because buying behavior is more predictable

Founder-market fit: the founder's unique combination of experience, network, and insight gives them an edge over other founders who might attempt the same market; Marc Andreessen argues this is more important than product-market fit early on
Willingness to pay (WTP) tests: the only way to know if someone will pay is to try to take their money; letters of intent, pre-orders, paid pilots, or actual cash collection beat all survey data

Market size red flags: a TAM slide showing 'we only need 1% of a 100 billion dollar market' is a warning sign; investors know that no one gets 1% of a huge market by accident, and the underlying analysis is usually wishful thinking

Founder biases: confirmation bias (seeking evidence that supports the idea), survivorship bias (studying winners without understanding the graveyard of failures), sunk cost fallacy (unwilling to kill ideas after investment); combat by writing down kill criteria in advance

Y Combinator kill criteria heuristic: if you cannot describe the problem and customer in one sentence each, if you cannot name 10 people who have the problem acutely, or if no one would pay today for a crappy version, the opportunity is probably not real
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run a live opportunity evaluation exercise on three real historical opportunities and rate each on a scorecard (market size, founder-market fit, WTP evidence, defensibility, timing). (1) Dropbox in 2007: Drew Houston was an MIT CS student, kept forgetting USB drives, built a crude demo video showing the product before it existed, posted to Hacker News, got 75,000 email signups overnight. (2) Theranos in 2003: Elizabeth Holmes dropped out of Stanford, had no medical or lab technology background, wanted to run hundreds of blood tests from a finger prick; scorecard is weak on founder-market fit, overestimated by investors who responded to the narrative over the underlying science. (3) Quibi in 2018: Jeffrey Katzenberg and Meg Whitman raised 1.75 billion dollars for premium short-form mobile video; scorecard shows weak WTP evidence and questionable painkiller claim. Students score each opportunity and discuss what evaluation would have revealed with rigor.
setup_requirements: Opportunity evaluation scorecard template (distributed as Google Sheet)
Hacker News and Product Hunt accounts for scanning current startup launches
Personal opportunity journal to record 5 problems observed this week in daily life
Access to Crunchbase for checking funding rounds and team backgrounds

review.value: The best opportunities come from founders who have personally experienced the problem they are solving; Drew Houston, the Collison brothers, and Brian Armstrong all built products they desperately wanted to use themselves. If you do not live the problem, be extremely skeptical that you will understand it deeply enough to solve it well

Tax software, payroll systems, and infrastructure tools consistently outperform content and social apps in reliable revenue because urgent pain drives willingness to pay; be suspicious of 'it would be cool if' opportunities that lack clear pain and urgency

Drew Houston's video MVP for Dropbox validated demand for 75,000 signups before he wrote the production code; this is the gold standard for opportunity testing. If you cannot get someone to express real interest in a crappy demo, they will not pay for the polished product either

Before investing in an opportunity, write down the specific evidence that would convince you to abandon it; this combats the sunk cost and confirmation biases that kill more startups than bad markets do. Review kill criteria monthly with intellectual honesty

class_schedule.topic: Customer Discovery and Problem Validation (Blank's Four Steps)
learning_objectives: Apply Steve Blank's Customer Development methodology with its four phases: Customer Discovery, Customer Validation, Customer Creation, Company Building
Design and conduct customer discovery interviews that elicit honest feedback rather than polite agreement
Distinguish problem interviews from solution interviews and know when each is appropriate
Avoid common customer discovery anti-patterns including pitching, leading questions, and the Mom Test failures
readings: Blank & Dorf, The Startup Owner's Manual (2012), Ch. 2-3 - Customer Discovery
Fitzpatrick, The Mom Test (2013), Ch. 1-3 - How to talk to customers when everyone is lying to you
Ries & Maurya, Running Lean (3rd ed.), Ch. 4 - Problem Validation
Blank, The Four Steps to the Epiphany (2005), Ch. 3 - Customer Discovery

lecture_notes.introduction: Steve Blank, who taught at Stanford and Berkeley after a career founding eight Silicon Valley companies, made one of the most influential observations in modern entrepreneurship: startups are not smaller versions of large companies and cannot be run using the same playbooks. A large company executes a known business model; a startup searches for one. This distinction matters because it changes everything about how you operate. Large-company playbooks assume you know your customer, the problem, the product, and the channel, so the work is execution. Startups begin with hypotheses about all four and must systematically test and refine them through direct contact with real customers. Blank calls this Customer Development, and the first phase (Customer Discovery) is where most founders either build the foundation for success or set themselves up for predictable failure. When Dropbox was in Y Combinator, Drew Houston spent weeks interviewing friends and colleagues about how they managed files across devices before building production code. Contrast this with Juicero, which raised 120 million dollars to build a 700 dollar wifi-connected juice press before discovering that Bloomberg journalists could squeeze the proprietary juice packs by hand faster than the machine did; the company collapsed in 2017.
key_concepts: Blank's Four Steps: Customer Discovery (find the problem and customer), Customer Validation (prove someone will pay), Customer Creation (scale demand generation), Company Building (scale the organization); each phase has specific exit criteria

Get Out of the Building (GOOB): Blank's catchphrase for the core discipline; you cannot validate hypotheses by talking to your team or running market research reports; you must talk directly to real prospective customers in their environment

Problem interviews: focused on understanding the customer's world, their current workarounds, what they have tried and failed, and what pain drives them; do not describe your solution yet, because you will bias the response
Solution interviews: only after the problem is validated; show mockups or demos, watch reactions, measure willingness to pre-order or sign a letter of intent
The Mom Test (Fitzpatrick): design interview questions that reveal truth even when the respondent wants to be nice; ask about specific past behavior rather than hypothetical future behavior

Past behavior beats stated intent: 'Have you ever paid for software like this?' beats 'Would you pay for software like this?'; people overestimate future willingness to act and underestimate existing constraints

Pitching is not interviewing: the moment you start pitching, the interview is over; you are now in sales mode, and the customer will either agree politely or disengage; keep the pitch out of the first 30 minutes

Leading questions destroy data: 'Don't you think it would be great if you could track your workouts automatically?' guarantees a yes and tells you nothing; open-ended questions like 'How do you currently track your workouts?' produce real data

Sample size heuristic: Blank recommends 20-30 interviews per customer segment before drawing conclusions; below 10 you are sampling noise, above 30 you often see diminishing returns; pattern recognition emerges around 15-20

Exit criteria from Customer Discovery: you can describe the customer archetype in specific demographic and behavioral terms, you can articulate the problem in the customer's own words, and you have at least a few customers willing to pay for or commit to a solution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop two interview scripts for the same hypothetical startup (a meal planning app for busy parents). BAD SCRIPT: 'Hi, I'm building an app that uses AI to help busy parents plan healthier meals. Would you use something like that? How much would you pay per month?' Problems: it pitches before listening, asks about hypothetical future behavior, leads the witness. GOOD SCRIPT: 'I'm researching how families handle dinner planning. Can you walk me through what happened last night? Who decided what to make, when did that decision happen? Have you ever used any apps to help with this? What made you stop? What is the most frustrating part of this for you?' This reveals the real workflow, existing tools tried, and whether this is a painkiller problem.
setup_requirements: Interview script template (Mom Test-compliant) distributed as Google Doc
Consent form template for recording interviews (audio only, respondent permission)
Access to calendar scheduling tool (Calendly free tier) for coordinating 5 practice interviews
Note-taking template with separate columns for facts, emotions, and quotes

review.value: You cannot validate startup hypotheses from inside your office. Real insight comes from direct, unmediated conversations with real prospective customers in their environment. Every founder who skips this step pays for it later with a product no one wants

People dramatically overestimate what they will do and underestimate their existing constraints. Ask 'Tell me about the last time you did X' rather than 'Would you do X?'. The Mom Test rule is: anything said about the future is an optimistic lie

The moment you start selling, you stop learning. In a one-hour interview, spend 55 minutes on their world and 5 minutes showing what you are considering. Early-stage discovery is about understanding the problem so well that the solution becomes obvious

Pattern recognition requires volume. Two interviews produce anecdote; 20 produce data. Commit to reaching 20-30 per segment before making big decisions, and review patterns weekly to identify emerging themes across interviews

learning_objectives: Construct a complete Business Model Canvas using Osterwalder's nine building blocks: customer segments, value propositions, channels, customer relationships, revenue streams, key resources, key activities, key partnerships, and cost structure
Analyze how each block relates to the others and identify internal inconsistencies that would break the business model
Reconstruct the historical canvases of successful startups (Airbnb, Netflix, Stripe) to learn from real examples
Use the canvas as a hypothesis-tracking tool, distinguishing validated blocks from untested assumptions
readings: Osterwalder & Pigneur, Business Model Generation (2010), Ch. 1-2 - Canvas and Patterns
Ries & Maurya, Running Lean (3rd ed.), Ch. 3 - Lean Canvas
Blank, 'Why the Lean Start-Up Changes Everything', Harvard Business Review (2013)
Strategyzer resources, 'Business Model Canvas Masterclass' (strategyzer.com)

lecture_notes.introduction: Alex Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas, published in Business Model Generation in 2010, did something that had never been done effectively before: it gave entrepreneurs and executives a single-page visual language for describing how a business actually creates, delivers, and captures value. Before the canvas, the dominant artifact for describing a business was the 40-page business plan, which required weeks to write, was almost always wrong in predictable ways, and was rarely read after the first funding round. The canvas replaces that with nine interconnected blocks that force you to be specific about your customers, how you reach them, what you sell them, how you earn money, and what you need to build and operate. Crucially, each block is a hypothesis, not a commitment; the canvas is meant to be updated as you learn. When Airbnb was founded, their initial canvas had 'conference attendees during sold-out events' as the primary customer segment; by the time they went public in 2020, their customer segment was global travelers and hosts in 220+ countries, with a canvas that looked nothing like the original. The canvas tracked the evolution of their business model in a way that no written business plan could. This session teaches you to build a canvas for your own venture, read canvases of real companies, and treat each block as a hypothesis to be tested.
key_concepts: Customer Segments: who are you creating value for and who are your most important customers; be specific enough that you can find and talk to real examples, not demographic abstractions
Value Propositions: what bundle of products and services creates value for each segment; describe in terms of the customer's problem or job-to-be-done, not your product features
Channels: how you reach customers to deliver the value proposition; includes awareness, evaluation, purchase, delivery, and post-sale support; direct vs indirect, owned vs partner
Customer Relationships: the type of relationship you establish and maintain (personal assistance, self-service, automated, communities, co-creation); this drives retention economics
Revenue Streams: how the business captures value from each segment; transaction, subscription, usage-based, licensing, advertising, brokerage fees; each has very different growth dynamics
Key Resources: the most important assets required to make the model work (physical, intellectual, human, financial); for a software startup this is often the engineering team and the codebase
Key Activities: the most important things the company must do well (production, problem solving, platform/network); these should map directly to delivering the value proposition

Key Partnerships: the network of suppliers and partners that make the model work; strategic alliances, coopetition, buyer-supplier relationships; Stripe's partnership with banks and card networks was existential
Cost Structure: the most important costs incurred; fixed vs variable, economies of scale, economies of scope; drives the P&L shape and unit economics

Lean Canvas (Maurya adaptation): replaces key activities, resources, and partnerships with problem, solution, key metrics, and unfair advantage; designed specifically for startup search mode rather than established business description
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build Airbnb's 2008 launch canvas vs 2020 IPO canvas side by side. 2008 canvas: Customer Segments: conference attendees in cities with sold-out hotels, apartment dwellers with spare space. Value Proposition: cheaper alternative to hotels, more local experience; for hosts, earn money from unused space. Channels: craigslist cross-posting, word of mouth, airbedandbreakfast.com. Revenue Streams: booking commission (initially very low to drive growth). Key Activities: trust and safety, matching, photography. Key Resources: the website and the community of early hosts. Cost Structure: small team, servers, customer support. 2020 canvas: Customer Segments: leisure travelers, business travelers, long-stay renters, hosts in 220 countries. Value Proposition: unique local experiences, belong anywhere, more than hotel. Channels: global search, SEO, performance marketing, referral, app, social. Revenue Streams: booking fees from both guests and hosts, experiences, Airbnb Plus. Key Activities: trust and safety (massive), platform engineering, compliance. Key Resources: brand, two-sided network, data, engineering team. Key Partnerships: governments, insurance providers, professional hosts. Cost Structure: heavy technology, safety and trust operations, marketing. Discuss what changed and why.
setup_requirements: Business Model Canvas template (print or Figma)
Strategyzer free account to use interactive canvas tool
Case study materials for Airbnb, Netflix, and Stripe canvas reconstructions
Whiteboard or Miro for group canvas exercise

review.value: Every block on your canvas is a hypothesis to be tested, not a commitment to execute. The goal of early-stage work is to test and update the canvas rapidly as you learn, not to defend an initial vision that was necessarily wrong in ways you did not yet understand

The blocks must make sense together. A premium value proposition paired with a low-cost mass-market channel, or a high-touch customer relationship paired with a razor-thin cost structure, is internally inconsistent and will break under stress. Check every pair of adjacent blocks for coherence

Write blocks in specific terms ('early-stage SaaS companies with 10-50 employees that struggle with HR compliance') rather than abstract ones ('SMBs that need help'). Specific segments can be found and interviewed; abstract ones cannot

Every successful startup's canvas looks dramatically different at IPO than at founding. Airbnb, Netflix, and Amazon all iterated their business models continuously. Plan to rebuild your canvas every quarter based on what you have learned from the market

learning_objectives: Apply Osterwalder's Value Proposition Canvas mapping customer jobs, pains, and gains to product pain relievers, gain creators, and the product or service itself
Distinguish functional, emotional, and social dimensions of customer jobs using the Jobs to Be Done framework
Craft value propositions that clearly articulate the customer problem, the distinctive solution, and the evidence of differentiation
Evaluate real value propositions from successful and failed startups and identify what made them resonate or fail
readings: Osterwalder et al., Value Proposition Design (2014), Ch. 1-3 - Canvas, Customer Profile, Value Map
Christensen, Hall, Dillon & Duncan, Competing Against Luck (2016), Ch. 1-3 - Jobs Theory
Ries, The Lean Startup (2011), Ch. 5 - Leap of Faith Assumptions
Harvard Business Review, Anthony Ulwick, 'Turn Customer Input into Innovation' (2002)

lecture_notes.introduction: Value proposition design is where most startups fail quietly. A venture can have the right team, the right market, and even the right technology, and still fail because the value proposition does not connect clearly to a customer job the customer actually wants done. Clayton Christensen's Jobs to Be Done framework argues that customers do not buy products; they hire products to do a job. When someone buys a milkshake at a fast food drive-through on the way to work, the functional job might be 'something to consume,' but the real job is 'make my boring morning commute more interesting and keep me full until lunch.' A competitor trying to steal milkshake market share with a healthier juice misunderstands the job; the real competitors are bagels, bananas, and boredom. Warby Parker's famous launch understood the job-to-be-done for its customers: 'Try on glasses without the hassle, pain, and markup of the traditional optical industry.' The Home Try-On program let customers order five frames, try them at home for five days, and return the ones they did not want, completely free. This was not just a feature; it was a direct answer to a real customer pain. The value proposition canvas, developed by Osterwalder and team, gives you a structured way to move from understanding the customer's world to designing a value proposition that fits. This session teaches you to build that fit explicitly, rather than hoping your product will resonate after launch.
key_concepts: Value Proposition Canvas: two sides - Customer Profile (jobs, pains, gains) on the right, Value Map (pain relievers, gain creators, products/services) on the left; achieve fit by matching them rigorously
Customer Jobs: functional (practical tasks), emotional (feelings desired), social (how customer wants to be seen by others); all three dimensions matter for most purchases

Customer Pains: negative emotions, costs, risks, and undesirable situations that the customer experiences before, during, or after trying to get a job done; the more extreme and frequent, the more valuable pain relief

Customer Gains: outcomes and benefits the customer wants; required gains (expected), expected gains (standard), desired gains (would be nice), unexpected gains (delight); focus on required and desired

Jobs to Be Done (Christensen): customers 'hire' products to do a job; understanding the job separates you from competitors who think they are in the same category as you but are actually hired for different jobs

The Milkshake Story: Christensen's famous study found morning milkshake buyers had a very different job-to-be-done than afternoon buyers; the insight transformed McDonald's milkshake strategy from categories to jobs

Pain relievers: specific ways your product eliminates or reduces customer pains; make them concrete and measurable (saves 2 hours per week, reduces cost by 30 percent, eliminates the need for training)
Gain creators: specific ways your product produces gains; match to required and desired gains, not just ones that are easy for you to deliver
Fit and resonance: fit is achieved when pains align with pain relievers and gains align with gain creators; without fit, you have features but no value proposition

Value proposition statement format: 'For [target customer] who [problem or need], our [product] is a [category] that [key benefit]. Unlike [competitor], we [differentiation].' Geoffrey Moore's positioning template
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a value proposition canvas for Warby Parker at launch in 2010. Customer jobs: functional (get corrective eyewear that works), emotional (feel good about how I look, feel informed and in control), social (express personal style, be perceived as current/modern). Customer pains: optical industry overpriced (most frames marked up 10-20x), intimidating store experience, limited selection, time-consuming, anxiety about appearance choice. Customer gains: style confidence, affordability, ease, ethical story (buy one give one). Warby Parker's value map: pain relievers - $95 glasses instead of $500+, try on 5 frames at home for free, buy one give one offsets ethical concern, free shipping. Gain creators - curated fashion-forward styles, storytelling around independent brand, easy return. Product/service - prescription glasses ordered online, Home Try-On program, lifestyle branding. Discuss why this value proposition was 10x better than the incumbent (LensCrafters, Pearle Vision, Luxottica) and how the Home Try-On was THE pain reliever that made the model work. Compare to Kozmo.com (dot-com era delivery startup), whose value proposition of 'one-hour delivery of DVD rentals and snacks' sounded good but did not map to a clear job-to-be-done that justified the delivery economics.
setup_requirements: Value Proposition Canvas template from Strategyzer
Printed or digital Jobs To Be Done interview script
Examples of strong and weak value propositions from real startups for comparison
Sticky notes or digital equivalent for collaborative canvas exercise

review.value: A value proposition without fit is just features. Fit means your pain relievers match real pains and your gain creators match real gains that customers have actually told you they want. Without fit, you are guessing, and most guesses are wrong

Customers do not buy features; they hire products to do jobs. Understanding the job requires looking at functional, emotional, and social dimensions. Competitors often misread which job a product is being hired for and lose as a result

Mild pains rarely drive purchase; extreme and frequent pains do. Warby Parker's 10x price reduction addressed an extreme pain (expensive glasses) with a concrete pain reliever. If your value proposition addresses only mild pains, your conversion will be weak

A value proposition can be tested with a landing page and ads before any product exists. If the landing page converts visitors to email signups or pre-orders, the value proposition is resonating. If it does not, no engineering effort will fix that

learning_objectives: Calculate Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Addressable Market, and Serviceable Obtainable Market using both top-down and bottom-up approaches
Identify and reject common market sizing errors including hand-wavy top-down calculations and unfounded capture rate assumptions
Use bottom-up market sizing to produce credible numbers investors will actually trust
Evaluate published TAM claims from real S-1 filings and pitch decks for plausibility
readings: Blank & Dorf, The Startup Owner's Manual (2012), Ch. 3 - Customer Validation (market sizing section)
Thiel & Masters, Zero to One (2014), Ch. 5 - Last Mover Advantage
Feld & Mendelson, Venture Deals (5th ed.), Ch. 1 - The Players (market sizing for VC pitches)
CB Insights, 'The Top 20 Reasons Startups Fail' (reference for 'no market need')

lecture_notes.introduction: Market sizing is the topic that separates credible founders from amateurs. Every VC has seen hundreds of pitch decks with a slide that says 'TAM: $500 billion' followed by 'We only need 1 percent to be a unicorn,' and every VC knows this is the fastest way to lose credibility. The problem is not the ambition; the problem is the reasoning. A top-down TAM is a number; a bottom-up SOM is an argument. Investors want to see that you have thought through who your actual customer is, how many of them exist, how much they spend, and how you plan to reach them. When Airbnb raised their Series A in 2011, their market sizing did not claim 1 percent of the 500 billion dollar global hospitality industry; instead, they built a bottom-up estimate starting from specific segments (budget travelers, unique experience seekers) and specific geographies (urban cores of global cities), then calculated realistic capture scenarios. Investors found this credible because it could be pressure-tested and validated against real data. This session teaches you to build market sizing that can withstand scrutiny, calculate the three levels (TAM, SAM, SOM) with both top-down and bottom-up approaches, and read published market sizing claims with healthy skepticism.
key_concepts: Total Addressable Market (TAM): the total annual revenue opportunity if every possible customer bought the product at the full price; the theoretical ceiling, useful for establishing whether the opportunity is large enough to justify VC investment
Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM): the portion of TAM you can actually reach given your business model, geography, regulatory constraints, and segment focus; always smaller than TAM

Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM): the portion of SAM you can realistically capture in a defined time horizon (typically 3-5 years) given competition and execution capacity; this is the number that actually matters for go-to-market planning

Top-down sizing: start with an industry report number (e.g., global hospitality = 500 billion), estimate your share; fast but rarely credible because the underlying report definition usually does not match your actual market
Bottom-up sizing: count actual customers times average spend per customer; requires you to know who your customer is in concrete terms, which forces the discipline of knowing your business

Example bottom-up (SaaS): 50,000 US SaaS companies x estimated 3 percent that fit our ICP = 1,500 ICP accounts; average contract value 15,000/year x 1,500 = 22.5M SAM; with 20 percent market share achievable over 5 years = 4.5M SOM
The 1 percent fallacy: 'we only need 1 percent of the market' is a red flag because no one wins 1 percent by accident; you either win a specific segment decisively or you do not win at all
Market growth rate: a 5 billion TAM growing at 20 percent per year is more attractive than a 15 billion TAM declining at 3 percent per year; investors want the market to be expanding

Early market vs mainstream market (Moore's Crossing the Chasm): early adopters are a fraction of the TAM; reaching the mainstream requires different product positioning, channels, and reference customers

Bottoms-up validation against real data: cross-check bottom-up estimates against public companies' revenue in your space, research firm reports, and pilot results; if your numbers are 10x off from public companies, something is wrong
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a market sizing exercise for a hypothetical B2B SaaS company selling compliance software to community banks. Top-down fail: 'Banking IT spending is 270 billion globally, we only need 0.1 percent.' This is useless because banking IT spending includes core banking, ATMs, fraud, analytics - most of which is not compliance software. Bottom-up approach: Step 1 - count the addressable customers. There are approximately 4,100 FDIC-insured banks in the US; exclude the 20 biggest (they build their own compliance tools), leaving 4,080 addressable. Step 2 - estimate willingness to pay: research public filings, find that community banks typically spend 50-200K per year on compliance software. Use 100K midpoint. Step 3 - TAM = 4,080 x 100,000 = 408 million. Step 4 - SAM: exclude credit unions, savings institutions, and banks under 100M in assets; remaining qualified market is 1,900 banks; SAM = 1,900 x 100,000 = 190 million. Step 5 - SOM in 5 years: realistic market share 10 percent = 19 million ARR, which implies roughly 190 customers. This is a credible number you can defend line by line, unlike the top-down fantasy.
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet template for market sizing calculations
FDIC, Census, or industry data sources for customer counts
Sample pitch decks with TAM slides (good and bad examples) for critique
Access to public filings (S-1, 10-K) for cross-checking with real companies

review.value: Bottom-up market sizing is harder to produce but dramatically more credible. Every investor will pressure-test your TAM, and top-down numbers collapse under pressure while bottom-up numbers can be defended line by line

No startup wins 1 percent of a huge market by accident. Investors know this and discount any pitch that leans on 'we only need 1 percent' language. Instead, show how you will win a specific segment decisively

review.key: SOM Is the Number That Matters
review.value: TAM sets the ambition ceiling, but SOM is what you are building toward in the near term. SOM should be credible, defensible, and reachable with the team and capital you have; it is the number that actually drives planning

A growing 5B market is more valuable than a shrinking 15B market. Investors care about trajectory as much as absolute size; ensure your market is expanding and articulate why (regulatory, demographic, technological drivers)

learning_objectives: Map the competitive landscape for a venture using direct competitors, indirect competitors, and substitutes, including current non-consumption
Apply Porter's Five Forces framework to assess structural attractiveness of the target market
Build competitive differentiation positioning that is credible, specific, and defensible (not just 'better and cheaper')
Identify defensibility mechanisms including network effects, switching costs, brand, economies of scale, and embedded data moats
readings: Thiel & Masters, Zero to One (2014), Ch. 3-5 - Competition, Monopoly, Last Mover Advantage
Porter, 'The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy', Harvard Business Review (2008)
Christensen, The Innovator's Dilemma (1997), Ch. 3-4 - Disruptive Technology and Value Networks
Hamilton Helmer, 7 Powers (2016), Introduction and Ch. 1-2 - Strategy as Power

lecture_notes.introduction: Peter Thiel's Zero to One contains one of the most contrarian claims in entrepreneurship: competition is for losers. His argument is that valuable businesses are built on some form of monopoly (in the technical sense, not the antitrust sense), because businesses in perfectly competitive markets cannot capture enough value to survive. Google is a monopoly in search and therefore immensely profitable; airlines are in a brutally competitive market and collectively have lost money over their history. The lesson for founders is not to avoid competition but to build a business in a domain where you can achieve durable differentiation. Most early-stage founders make two opposite mistakes: they deny that competitors exist ('we have no competitors') or they list every company vaguely in their space ('we compete with Microsoft, Google, Salesforce, and Oracle'). Both responses miss the real competitive question: who do customers actually consider when they are solving the problem you address, and why would they choose you? Stripe's 'competitor' at launch was not PayPal Payments Pro; it was Braintree, Authorize.net, and the customer's own current workaround (often accepting payments via invoice). Stripe beat those competitors not by being cheaper, but by being dramatically easier to integrate for developers, building an actual differentiation moat. This session teaches you to map competitors accurately, analyze market structure, and define a position you can defend.
key_concepts: Direct competitors: companies selling similar solutions to the same customers for the same job; examples are obvious but sometimes missed when founders deny competition
Indirect competitors: companies solving the same customer job with a different approach; Slack competes with email, not just HipChat and Microsoft Teams
Substitutes: any way the customer currently solves the problem, including doing nothing; the status quo is almost always your biggest competitor because it has zero switching cost

Non-consumption (Christensen): a huge opportunity exists in customers who currently cannot or do not consume the category at all; disruptive innovations often target non-consumption first, then move upmarket

Porter's Five Forces: threat of new entrants, bargaining power of buyers, bargaining power of suppliers, threat of substitutes, intensity of rivalry; structurally unattractive markets make it hard to succeed regardless of execution

Differentiation vs low cost: Porter's generic strategies distinguish these as two viable positions; trying to do both (stuck in the middle) usually fails; startups almost always pursue differentiation because they cannot win a cost war against incumbents

Defensibility mechanisms (Helmer's 7 Powers): scale economies, network effects, switching costs, counter-positioning, cornered resources, process power, branding; identify which ones your business can credibly build
Network effects: value of the product increases with the number of users; Facebook, Uber, Airbnb, and eBay all built network effect moats that made them nearly impossible to displace once established
Switching costs: costs (time, money, risk) that customers incur to leave you; enterprise SaaS like Salesforce builds switching costs through data, customizations, integrations, and training

Positioning statement: specific, customer-centric claim about how you differ from alternatives; not 'we are the best' but 'we are the only CRM purpose-built for inside sales teams with SMS and calling integrated natively'
lecture_notes.demonstration: Map the competitive landscape for Stripe at its 2011 launch. Direct competitors: PayPal Payments Pro, Braintree, Authorize.net, WePay. Indirect competitors: using a traditional merchant account through a bank, PayPal Standard checkout (redirects to PayPal), Google Checkout. Substitutes: invoicing customers and waiting for bank transfers, requiring check payment, not accepting online payments at all. Non-consumption: the vast number of small developers and startups that found existing payment options so painful they simply did not accept online payments yet. Stripe's insight was that this last segment was both large and underserved. The incumbents had optimized for enterprise sales to merchants and treated developers as an afterthought. Stripe's differentiation was a developer-first experience: signup in 5 minutes with just an email, 7 lines of code to integrate, clean documentation, and a reasonable pricing model (2.9 percent plus 30 cents). Apply Porter's Five Forces: new entrant threat is high (low capital requirements for software), buyer power is moderate (developers can switch), supplier power is high (card networks dictate interchange), substitute threat is high (PayPal), rivalry is intense. Yet Stripe built a durable position because of three powers: brand among developers, process power in integration simplicity, and scale economies in compliance and fraud infrastructure.
setup_requirements: Competitive landscape map template (2x2 positioning matrix)
Porter Five Forces worksheet
Helmer 7 Powers checklist
Access to G2, Capterra, or similar for identifying real competitors in a chosen space

review.value: The customer's current workaround, even if it is bad, is your biggest competitor because it has zero switching cost and no decision pain. If you cannot articulate why the customer will switch from doing nothing, you do not have a business yet

Startups almost never win by being cheaper than incumbents; incumbents have cost advantages you cannot match. Win by being differentiated in a way that matters to a specific segment, then expand from there

Network effects, switching costs, and process power do not exist on day one; they are built through deliberate product and business model choices. Identify which moats your business can build and invest in them from the beginning

Christensen's research shows disruptive innovations typically start by serving non-consumers or over-served customers that incumbents ignore. This is why Stripe started with developers and Airbnb started with conference attendees rather than trying to fight Marriott directly

class_schedule.topic: Lean Startup Methodology: Build-Measure-Learn and MVPs (Ries)
learning_objectives: Apply Eric Ries's Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop to accelerate validated learning and reduce wasted effort
Design Minimum Viable Products that test the riskiest hypothesis using the cheapest acceptable fidelity
Recognize MVP types (concierge, Wizard of Oz, landing page, video, prototype) and choose the right one for a given hypothesis
Apply innovation accounting using pirate metrics (AARRR) or similar frameworks to measure real progress
readings: Ries, The Lean Startup (2011), Ch. 5-9 - Vision, Steer, Leap, Test, Measure
Maurya, Running Lean (3rd ed.), Ch. 5-7 - Experimentation and Validation
Blank, 'Why the Lean Startup Changes Everything', Harvard Business Review (2013)
First Round Review, 'The Dropbox Story' and 'The Airbnb Story' case studies

lecture_notes.introduction: The Lean Startup method, formalized by Eric Ries in 2011, argues that startups exist to maximize validated learning about customers and markets per unit of time and capital. Traditional engineering and business school training emphasizes planning and execution, but startups operate under conditions of extreme uncertainty where the initial plan is almost always wrong. The response is not to plan more carefully but to learn faster. The Build-Measure-Learn loop turns ideas into products, products into data, and data into decisions that either validate or invalidate the original hypotheses. The goal is to minimize the total time through the loop, because that determines how quickly you can find a working business model before you run out of money. Drew Houston at Dropbox demonstrated this brilliantly: instead of spending six months building a sync engine and launching it to silence, he spent a weekend making a three-minute demo video explaining the product and posted it to Hacker News. The video (which showed features that did not exist yet, narrated in a way tailored to the exact subculture of early adopters) drove 75,000 signups for the waitlist, providing validated learning that the problem was real and the value proposition resonated before any meaningful engineering was done. This session teaches you to design experiments that produce learning, not theater; to build MVPs that are minimally viable but actually test hypotheses; and to measure in ways that prevent self-deception.
key_concepts: Build-Measure-Learn loop: ideas -> build MVP -> measure user behavior -> learn -> iterate; optimize the loop duration, not the quality of the individual build
Minimum Viable Product: the smallest product you can launch that begins the learning loop with real customers; not the smallest product you would be willing to ship to everyone
Concierge MVP: manually deliver the service to a small number of customers to validate demand and learn workflow before building software; Food on the Table famously started this way

Wizard of Oz MVP: front-end looks like a product but humans do the work behind the scenes; Zappos founder Nick Swinmurn bought shoes at local stores after customers ordered online, proving the demand before building inventory
Landing page MVP: a single web page describing the product with a call to action (email signup, pre-order); measures conversion rate from interested visitors, validating value proposition resonance
Video MVP: Dropbox's famous 3-minute demo video that drove 75,000 signups before the product existed; works when the product is hard to describe in text but easy to show
Pivot vs persevere: the most important decision in a startup; a pivot is a structured change to one element of the business model based on validated learning, not a random change of direction

Innovation accounting: a system for measuring progress in a startup where traditional accounting (revenue, profit) is not yet meaningful; uses leading indicators to show whether hypotheses are being validated
AARRR Pirate Metrics (Dave McClure): Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue; track funnel conversion at each stage to identify where the business is leaking value

Vanity metrics vs actionable metrics: vanity metrics (total users, total visits) look good but do not drive decisions; actionable metrics (cohort retention, per-user revenue, per-channel CAC) reveal what is working
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Dropbox's video MVP in detail. In 2007 Drew Houston had a sync engine prototype but no polished product; he also had limited budget for development. Instead of building, he made a 3-minute screencast narrated in the voice and humor of the Hacker News / Digg community he was targeting, embedded inside jokes, and posted it. The video showed features that would exist in production (drag-and-drop sync, automatic backup, seamless cross-device access) with no live product behind it. Results: waitlist signups jumped from 5,000 to 75,000 overnight; the top comment on Hacker News called it 'the most useful product I have ever seen'; the video became the strongest possible validation that the problem was real and the solution was desired. The insight: the riskiest hypothesis was not 'can we build the sync engine' (they had one); it was 'will early adopters care.' The video tested that hypothesis for roughly the cost of a weekend. Compare to Color Labs, which raised 41 million dollars on a vague vision, spent nothing testing the hypothesis with real users, launched a confusing product, and shut down within two years. Same ecosystem, opposite approach, predictable outcomes.
setup_requirements: MVP experiment template (hypothesis, test, success criteria, cost)
Access to Unbounce, Carrd, or Webflow for building landing page MVPs
Typeform or Google Forms for concierge data collection
Analytics tool (Google Analytics, Plausible, or Mixpanel free tier)

review.value: The value produced by a startup in its earliest stage is not revenue or product; it is validated learning about whether the business model works. Optimize everything for learning speed per dollar, because that is what determines whether you find a working model before running out of capital

The MVP should test whatever hypothesis is most likely to kill the business. For Dropbox it was demand, for Stripe it was developer experience, for Airbnb it was trust between strangers. Do not build features that test things you already know

A concierge or Wizard of Oz MVP is not deception if the customer is getting value; it is just not using the automation you plan to build. This approach validates demand and workflow at a fraction of the cost of the polished product

Total signups, total visits, and social media followers feel good and tell investors a story, but they do not drive decisions. Track cohort retention, per-channel CAC, activation rate, and revenue per user to see whether the business actually works

class_schedule.topic: Financial Projections and Unit Economics for Startups
learning_objectives: Build a bottom-up financial projection model for a startup including revenue, costs, headcount, and cash burn
Calculate and interpret unit economics metrics: CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, payback period, and contribution margin
Distinguish healthy from unhealthy unit economics across SaaS, marketplace, e-commerce, and hardware business models
Model the relationship between burn rate, runway, and fundraising timing to avoid cash-out disasters
readings: Feld & Mendelson, Venture Deals (5th ed.), Ch. 2-3 - Terms, Economics and Control
David Skok, 'SaaS Metrics 2.0' (forentrepreneurs.com, 2013, updated)
Andreessen Horowitz, '16 Startup Metrics' (a16z.com essay)
Bill Gurley, 'The Dangerous Seduction of the LTV Formula' (abovethecrowd.com, 2012)

lecture_notes.introduction: Financial projections for startups are both essential and almost always wrong. They are essential because they force discipline about the assumptions behind your business model, expose the relationship between acquisition cost and lifetime value, and give investors a framework for evaluating capital efficiency. They are almost always wrong because the future is uncertain and early-stage startups have little historical data to anchor projections to. The solution is not to skip financial modeling but to treat it as an articulation of assumptions that can be tested and updated as you learn. The single most important concept in startup finance is unit economics: the revenue and cost associated with one customer. If your unit economics are unhealthy (you lose money on each customer acquired, or your payback period is longer than your runway), no amount of fundraising will fix the business. WeWork is the canonical cautionary tale: the company raised 10+ billion dollars from SoftBank and others while losing money on nearly every building it leased and operated. The unit economics never worked at scale, but the narrative of blitzscaling papered over the numbers until the 2019 S-1 disaster exposed them. This session teaches you to build simple, defensible financial models; calculate unit economics correctly; and understand the relationship between burn rate, runway, and fundraising timing that determines whether a startup survives.
key_concepts: Bottom-up revenue projection: number of customers x average revenue per customer, driven by realistic acquisition math (channels, conversion rates, ramp-up); top-down revenue projections like 'we will capture 1 percent of a 10B market' are worthless
Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC): total sales and marketing spend divided by new customers acquired in the same period; fully loaded CAC includes salaries, tools, and overhead, not just ad spend
Lifetime Value (LTV): the gross profit a customer generates over their entire relationship with the company; for SaaS, typically calculated as ARPU x gross margin / churn rate
LTV:CAC ratio: the efficiency of growth capital; 3:1 is the common benchmark for sustainable unit economics, 5:1 is excellent, below 1:1 means you lose money on each acquisition

CAC payback period: how long it takes to recover the acquisition cost through gross profit from that customer; SaaS target is 12-18 months, faster is better, longer than 24 months is often unsustainable
Contribution margin: revenue minus all variable costs (COGS, payment processing, support, shipping); the first-level measure of whether you make money per transaction
Burn rate: monthly cash consumption; gross burn is total monthly spend, net burn subtracts revenue; what determines how fast you run out of cash
Runway: cash on hand divided by net monthly burn; the number of months you have before you need to raise again or become profitable; typical guidance is to raise with 18+ months of runway

Blitzscaling unit economics trap (Gurley): SaaS companies often report impressive LTV:CAC ratios based on optimistic churn assumptions; if real churn is 2x projected churn, LTV collapses and the economics reverse

Default alive vs default dead (Paul Graham): a startup is default alive if, given current growth rate and runway, it can reach profitability before running out of cash; default dead if it cannot; most startups that raise too little are default dead and should either cut costs aggressively or raise more
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a sample unit economics model for a B2B SaaS company. Inputs: average contract value 12,000/year, gross margin 80 percent, monthly churn 2 percent, sales and marketing spend 500,000/quarter, new customers acquired 50/quarter. Calculations: CAC = 500,000 / 50 = 10,000. ARPU monthly = 1,000. Average customer lifetime = 1 / 0.02 = 50 months. LTV = 1,000 x 0.80 x 50 = 40,000. LTV:CAC = 40,000 / 10,000 = 4:1 (healthy). CAC payback = 10,000 / (1,000 x 0.80) = 12.5 months (healthy). Now run the scenario where churn is actually 4 percent monthly (a common surprise as the company scales). Lifetime = 25 months. LTV = 20,000. LTV:CAC = 2:1 (marginal). Payback unchanged at 12.5 months because it depends on acquisition, not lifetime. Conclusion: churn matters enormously, and optimism about retention can flip the business from healthy to unhealthy without anyone noticing until the cohort data is mature. Compare to a marketplace model where take rate, not ARPU, drives revenue, and to a D2C model where repeat purchase rate dominates LTV.
setup_requirements: Excel or Google Sheets financial model template with revenue, costs, headcount, and cash flow
SaaS Metrics template from David Skok's site
Access to public SaaS company financials (Shopify, HubSpot, Zoom) for benchmarking
CAC and LTV calculator spreadsheet (distributed in class)

review.value: If your unit economics do not work at scale, no amount of fundraising will rescue the business. Prove you make money on each customer before scaling acquisition; WeWork is the most expensive cautionary tale on what happens when you ignore this

review.key: Payback Matters as Much as LTV
review.value: LTV:CAC of 5:1 with a 36-month payback period is worse than LTV:CAC of 3:1 with a 12-month payback period, because payback dictates cash flow timing and therefore fundraising pressure. Short payback enables reinvestment and compounds

Early churn rates almost always understate true long-term churn because the first cohorts are highly selected early adopters. Stress-test your LTV by modeling 2x projected churn and confirming the business still works

Raise when you have 12-18 months of runway left, not when you are about to run out; desperate fundraising produces bad terms or failed rounds. Plan fundraising timing as a strategic activity with a 6-month lead time

class_schedule.topic: Funding Landscape I: Bootstrapping, Friends & Family, Angels, Crowdfunding
learning_objectives: Compare bootstrapping, friends and family, angel investment, and crowdfunding as capital sources and identify when each is appropriate
Understand the economics of bootstrapped companies like Mailchimp, Basecamp, and pre-acquisition GitHub that reached substantial scale without venture capital
Navigate the process of raising an angel round including finding investors, pitching, and closing a SAFE or convertible note
Evaluate crowdfunding models (Kickstarter rewards, Indiegogo, equity crowdfunding under Reg CF) and the companies that have used them successfully
readings: Jason Fried & DHH, Rework (2010), Ch. on Starting and Bootstrapping
Brad Feld & Jason Mendelson, Venture Deals (5th ed.), Ch. 1-2 - The Players and Key Terms
Paul Graham, 'How to Fund a Startup' and 'Default Alive or Default Dead' essays
AngelList, 'State of Angel Investing' reports (annual)

lecture_notes.introduction: The decision about how to fund a venture is often framed as 'VC or nothing,' but this framing is wrong and destructive. Most successful small businesses are bootstrapped, and several iconic technology companies either bootstrapped entirely or raised minimal capital. Mailchimp, founded in 2001 by Ben Chestnut and Dan Kurzius, grew to over 600 million dollars in annual revenue and 1,000 employees entirely without outside investment before being acquired by Intuit in 2021 for 12 billion dollars. Basecamp (37signals), founded by Jason Fried and David Heinemeier Hansson, has remained profitable and independent for over 20 years, with Fried and DHH writing widely influential books (Rework, Getting Real) about why most startups should not raise venture capital. GitHub operated for four years on founder savings and customer revenue before raising its first VC round in 2012; it was acquired by Microsoft in 2018 for 7.5 billion dollars. These examples are not arguments against VC; they are arguments for matching your funding strategy to your business model, growth needs, and personal goals. VC capital is appropriate for businesses that need to reach massive scale quickly to win network effects or winner-take-all dynamics. It is inappropriate for profitable-from-day-one businesses where the founders want control and long-term optionality. This session covers the full capital stack below VC: founder savings, friends and family, angel investors, crowdfunding, and revenue-based financing.
key_concepts: Bootstrapping: funding the business from founder savings, founder salaries foregone, and customer revenue; forces discipline about unit economics because there is no external capital to paper over bad economics

Friends and family round: small amounts (typically under 250K) from people who know the founders personally; governed by securities regulations even when informal; document carefully to avoid legal problems later

Angel investors: high-net-worth individuals investing personal capital, typically 25K to 1M per check; provide capital plus sometimes advice, introductions, and validation; AngelList has made this much more accessible

Super angels and micro VCs: individuals or small funds writing many small checks (10-500K) at the seed stage; examples include Ron Conway's SV Angel, Naval Ravikant's AngelList, and Jason Calacanis's LAUNCH

SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity): Y Combinator's standard instrument for seed investment; provides the investor with the right to shares in a future priced round at a discount or cap, without setting a price today

Convertible notes: debt instruments that convert to equity at a future priced round; similar to SAFEs but include interest and maturity dates; falling out of favor relative to SAFEs for founder-friendliness

Reward-based crowdfunding (Kickstarter, Indiegogo): backers pre-order a product; success cases include Pebble watch (20M raised), Oculus (2.4M raised before Facebook acquisition), Exploding Kittens (8.7M raised)

Equity crowdfunding (Regulation CF): since 2016, U.S. startups can raise up to 5 million per year from retail investors through platforms like Republic, StartEngine, Wefunder; lower friction than traditional equity but brings many small shareholders

Revenue-based financing: alternative capital where you repay a percentage of monthly revenue until a multiple (typically 1.5-2.5x) is reached; avoids dilution and works for profitable growing businesses; providers include Lighter Capital, Clearco, Pipe

Grants and non-dilutive: NSF SBIR, NIH SBIR, state economic development programs, corporate innovation grants; competitive but do not require equity or repayment; particularly relevant for deep tech and biotech
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examine Mailchimp's bootstrapped trajectory as a counter-example to the VC default. Ben Chestnut and Dan Kurzius started Mailchimp in 2001 as a side project inside their web design agency. For the first eight years they ran it as a profitable side business while the agency paid the bills, gradually adding features and customers. In 2009 they made the pivotal decision to offer a free plan, which generated dramatic growth (from 85,000 users to 450,000 in one year). They never raised venture capital, instead funding every expansion from cash flow. Key operating principles: hire slowly, price for profit not growth, ship features based on customer feedback, and refuse to pursue the pivot-of-the-month even as competitors raised hundreds of millions. By the time of acquisition in 2021, Mailchimp had over 13 million users and roughly 800 million in ARR, acquired for 12 billion dollars by Intuit. Chestnut and Kurzius collected the full proceeds personally, with no dilution from investor ownership. Compare to a VC-backed email marketing competitor that raised four rounds totaling 60 million dollars and eventually sold for less than the total raised, leaving founders with a small fraction of the exit value. Both outcomes are legitimate paths, but the contrast reveals how funding structure shapes founder outcomes.
setup_requirements: Y Combinator SAFE template (downloadable from yc.com)
Spreadsheet for modeling bootstrapped vs VC-funded cap table scenarios
Access to Kickstarter, Wefunder, and AngelList for case study analysis
Crunchbase account for looking up fundraising histories

review.value: Funding strategy should follow from business model requirements, not the reverse. Winner-take-all markets with strong network effects justify VC; niche profitable businesses usually do not. Getting this backwards is one of the most destructive founder mistakes

Bootstrapping forces discipline about unit economics and aligns incentives with customers rather than investors. Mailchimp, Basecamp, and GitHub demonstrate that massive outcomes are possible without VC, and that founders retain more equity at exit

For seed rounds under 2 million, SAFEs have replaced convertible notes as the default instrument. Familiarize yourself with discount, cap, and post-money SAFE mechanics; investors will expect you to know them

Pebble, Oculus, and Exploding Kittens demonstrate that reward-based crowdfunding can launch real companies; equity crowdfunding under Reg CF has grown into a legitimate capital source for non-VC-backable but profitable businesses. Do not dismiss it as marketing theater

class_schedule.topic: Funding Landscape II: Venture Capital, Term Sheets, and Dilution
learning_objectives: Understand how venture capital funds work including LP structure, fund lifecycle, return expectations, and the implications for portfolio company behavior
Read and interpret a standard Series A term sheet including economic and control terms
Calculate the dilution effect of multiple financing rounds on a founder cap table using pre-money and post-money math
Evaluate the implications of liquidation preferences, anti-dilution protection, board composition, and protective provisions
readings: Feld & Mendelson, Venture Deals (5th ed.), Ch. 3-6 - Economics, Control, Liquidation, Anti-dilution
Harvard Business Review, Bill Sahlman, 'How Venture Capitalists Evaluate Potential Venture Opportunities' (1997)
Paul Graham, 'The Equity Equation' and 'The High-Res Society' essays
Andreessen Horowitz, 'The VC Dilemma' blog posts

lecture_notes.introduction: Venture capital is the most misunderstood component of the startup ecosystem. Founders often view VCs as either angels or villains, but the reality is more structural: VCs are managing other people's money on a clock, with return expectations that shape every decision they make. A typical VC fund has limited partners (pension funds, endowments, family offices) who expect 3x+ returns over a 10-year fund life. With most portfolio investments returning nothing (the power law is brutal), the fund's total return depends on a small number of 10-100x winners. This math drives VC behavior: VCs need every portfolio company to have the potential to return the entire fund, which is why they pressure companies toward aggressive growth even when slower growth would be better for the business. Understanding this dynamic is essential to deciding whether VC is right for you and to negotiating term sheets that align incentives. Google's 1999 Series A from Sequoia and Kleiner Perkins set the tone for modern Silicon Valley venture capital: 25 million dollar investment at a 100 million post-money valuation, board seats for both firms, and standard preferences. The Google outcome (market cap over 2 trillion today) produced legendary returns for those firms, and set expectations for what founders could raise and what terms were considered 'founder-friendly.' This session walks through a real term sheet line by line, calculates dilution across multiple rounds, and shows how term structure affects founder outcomes at exit.
key_concepts: VC fund structure: general partners (GPs) manage the fund and receive management fees (typically 2 percent of committed capital per year) plus carried interest (typically 20 percent of profits above the hurdle); LPs provide capital
Fund lifecycle: 10 years typical, with investment period (years 1-4), active management (years 3-7), harvesting (years 6-10); this drives the pressure to exit portfolio companies within a decade

Power law returns: approximately 50 percent of VC investments lose money, 30 percent return capital, 20 percent produce the returns; the top 1 percent of investments produce most of the fund's return; this math justifies investor behavior that seems irrational at a single-company level

Pre-money vs post-money valuation: pre-money is the valuation before the new investment; post-money equals pre-money plus investment; percentage ownership for new investor is investment divided by post-money

Liquidation preference: the amount investors get before common shareholders in an exit; 1x non-participating is founder-friendly (investors get their money back or convert to common, whichever is greater); 1x participating is less friendly (investors get money back AND participate in common upside); multiple preferences (2x, 3x) are predatory

Anti-dilution protection: weighted average (standard, protects against a down round by adjusting conversion ratio fractionally) vs full ratchet (extreme, adjusts conversion to the new lowest price, devastating to founders); weighted average is standard in founder-friendly deals
Board composition: typical Series A has 5 seats - 2 founders, 2 investors, 1 independent; control shifts decisively away from founders in later rounds as additional investor seats are added

Protective provisions: actions requiring investor consent; standard list includes selling the company, raising debt, changing the cap table, approving budget; these effectively give investors veto power over major decisions

Option pool refresh: Series A term sheets typically require the company to create a new employee option pool (often 10-15 percent of post-money) before the investment, which comes out of the founders' existing equity; this is sometimes called the 'option pool shuffle' and is a hidden dilution mechanism

Sample cap table progression: founders start with 100 percent, after seed (raising 2M at 10M post) they have 80 percent, after Series A (10M at 40M post plus option pool) they might have 55 percent, after Series B (25M at 100M post) they might have 40 percent, by Series C founders are typically at 25-35 percent combined
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a sample cap table progression for a hypothetical startup through seed, Series A, and Series B. Starting point: two founders each own 50 percent, 10M shares total, no option pool. Seed round: raise 2M at 10M post-money on a SAFE with 10M cap. After conversion, SAFE holders own 20 percent, founders 80 percent (40/40). Pre-Series A: expand option pool to 10 percent of post-money (8 percent reduction to existing shareholders' percentages). Series A: raise 10M at 40M post-money (30M pre); new investors own 25 percent. After Series A: founders at roughly 55 percent combined (27.5/27.5), seed investors at 15 percent, option pool at 5 percent remaining available, Series A at 25 percent. Series B: raise 30M at 120M post-money (90M pre); new investors own 25 percent; existing shareholders diluted by 25 percent across the board. Founders at roughly 41 percent combined (20.5/20.5), Series A at 18.75 percent, seed at 11.25 percent, option pool replenished to 10 percent. At acquisition for 400M with 1x non-participating preference: Series B gets 30M back or 25 percent of 400M = 100M (takes the 100M), Series A gets 10M back or 18.75 percent = 75M (takes 75M), seed gets 2M back or 11.25 percent = 45M (takes 45M), option pool gets 10 percent = 40M, founders split remaining 140M at 70M each. The founders built a 400M company and walked away with 70M each after three years of intense work.
setup_requirements: Cap table template spreadsheet (Carta Launch free tier or Google Sheets)
Sample term sheets from Y Combinator, Cooley, and Wilson Sonsini
Access to Crunchbase or PitchBook for looking up real funding rounds
Calculator for pre-money vs post-money math

review.value: Because of the power law, every VC investment must have the potential to return the fund on its own. This is why VCs pressure companies toward aggressive growth even when slower growth would be better for the founders or the business; the math drives the behavior

review.key: Terms Matter as Much as Valuation
review.value: A higher valuation with bad terms (2x participating preference, full ratchet anti-dilution) can be worse than a lower valuation with clean terms. Focus on the terms not just the headline number; Venture Deals is the indispensable guide

Each round dilutes existing shareholders proportionally, and the option pool refresh is a hidden additional dilution. Founders who start at 100 percent typically end up at 10-30 percent combined by Series C; plan for this mathematically from the beginning

After Series B or C, founders are typically a minority on their own board. Understand that taking VC means gradually transferring control; if you want to run the company on your own terms long term, VC is not the right path

learning_objectives: Construct a pitch deck using the Sequoia template and the Kawasaki 10/20/30 rule
Tell a compelling investor story that integrates problem, market, solution, traction, team, and ask
Anticipate and address common investor objections and due diligence questions
Deliver the pitch with appropriate pacing, narrative, and responsiveness to investor signals
readings: Guy Kawasaki, 'The 10/20/30 Rule of PowerPoint' (guykawasaki.com)
Sequoia Capital, 'Writing a Business Plan' (sequoiacap.com)
Oren Klaff, Pitch Anything (2011), Ch. 1-4 - Frame Control and STRONG Method
Y Combinator Library, 'How to Pitch Your Startup' (Kevin Hale video transcript)

lecture_notes.introduction: The pitch deck is the most overrated and most underrated document in entrepreneurship simultaneously. It is overrated because founders spend enormous amounts of time polishing slides and obsessing over fonts when the underlying story is not yet clear; it is underrated because when the story is clear and the fundamentals are real, a 10-slide deck can raise millions of dollars in weeks. Guy Kawasaki's 10/20/30 rule (10 slides, 20 minutes, 30-point font minimum) codifies what experienced pitchers have learned: investors have short attention, their time is valuable, and the point of a pitch is not to explain everything but to generate enough interest for a follow-up meeting. The Sequoia template, published openly by Sequoia Capital based on decades of seeing thousands of pitches, recommends 10 specific sections in a specific order: company purpose, problem, solution, why now, market size, competition, product, business model, team, and financials. This template works because it matches the order investors think about opportunities, and deviating from it forces investors to reorder information in their heads, which is cognitively expensive.
