Airbnb's famous Series A deck, still publicly available online, is 15 slides and almost aggressively minimal; Chesky presented it and raised 600K in seed then 7.2M Series A, largely on the strength of a story that matched the Sequoia structure with clear numbers in each section. This session teaches you to build a pitch deck that actually works, tell the story verbally without reading slides, and handle the objections and diligence questions that follow.
key_concepts: Kawasaki 10/20/30 rule: no more than 10 slides, no more than 20 minutes of presentation, no font smaller than 30 points; forces brevity and highlights the essential story
Sequoia template sections: Company purpose (one sentence), Problem, Solution, Why now (timing), Market size, Competition, Product, Business model, Team, Financials and ask
Problem slide: articulate the customer pain in the customer's own words with a specific concrete example; avoid abstract language about 'pain points' and 'friction'
Solution slide: describe what you do in one sentence a non-expert can understand; show product screenshots, not just text description
Why now slide: explain the timing; what has changed in technology, regulation, or behavior that makes this opportunity possible now; the strongest pitches have an obvious 'why now' answer
Market size slide: bottom-up calculation with defensible assumptions; avoid hand-wavy top-down claims; include TAM, SAM, SOM with reasoning
Traction slide: the most important slide if you have traction; show revenue or usage growth chart, retention, and key customer logos; momentum beats everything else

Team slide: founder bios emphasizing relevant experience and founder-market fit; photos humanize the pitch; if your team is not the right team for this market, the pitch will fail regardless of other slides
Ask slide: amount raising, lead investor status, use of funds broken down by category; be specific about the milestones the round will achieve
Pitch delivery principles: tell a story not a list, speak to the slides not from them, maintain eye contact and pacing, anticipate questions and hold answers for Q&A, show passion without theatrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Study Airbnb's actual seed pitch deck slide by slide (widely available online). Slide 1: Airbnb - Book rooms with locals rather than hotels. One sentence, clear category. Slide 2: Problem - Price sensitive travelers have no alternatives to hotels; hotels disconnect you from the city; no easy way to book a room with a local. Concrete problem articulation. Slide 3: Solution - A web platform where users rent out their space to travelers. Clear simple solution. Slide 4: Market validation - trends in travel, couchsurfing (40K members), vacation rental industry (1.9B). Slide 5: Market size - 2B trips per year, 10.6M temporary housing offerings. Slide 6: Product - three steps (search, book, review) with screenshots. Slide 7: Business model - 10 percent commission per transaction. Slide 8: Market adoption - current users, cities, conferences served. Slide 9: Competition - positioning chart placing Airbnb against Craigslist, Couchsurfing, and vacation rental sites. Slide 10: Competitive advantages. Slide 11-15: Team, traction, advisors, press, financials, ask. Discuss what this deck did well (clear story, concrete numbers, defensible differentiation, strong founder-market fit story) and what it got away with (very speculative market size). The deck worked because the story was clean and the founders had genuine early traction they could point to.
setup_requirements: Sequoia pitch template (downloadable from sequoiacap.com)
Airbnb pitch deck example (slidebean.com/blog/airbnb-pitch-deck)
Figma or Google Slides for deck construction
Video recording setup for pitch practice and self-review

review.value: The slides are a supporting document for the story you tell in 20 minutes. If your story is unclear, no amount of slide polish will save the pitch; if your story is strong, even ugly slides will get you funded. Focus on the underlying narrative first

Every claim in the deck should be as specific as possible. 'Large market' is weak; 'we serve 4,080 U.S. community banks with average contract value of 100K' is strong. Specificity signals rigor; vagueness signals the opposite

If you have real traction (customers, revenue, usage growth), show it on the front page of the deck and structure the rest around amplifying that momentum. If you do not, you are selling a story and need to make the story unusually compelling on other dimensions

Practice the pitch until it feels boring to you; that is usually when it starts to feel polished to investors. Video yourself, watch critically, and iterate on pacing, transitions, and the specific phrasing of key lines. The best pitchers have rehearsed hundreds of times

class_schedule.topic: Hiring, Culture, and Scaling the Early Team
learning_objectives: Design an early hiring plan that matches roles to stage-appropriate needs and prioritizes founder coverage gaps
Apply Horowitz's thinking on 'CEO psychology' and making hard hiring, firing, and performance management decisions
Build intentional culture through rituals, values, and hiring filters rather than leaving culture to form by accident
Recognize the people dysfunctions that kill startups including founder conflict, key employee departures, and organizational chaos at 50-150 employees
readings: Ben Horowitz, The Hard Thing About Hard Things (2014), Ch. 4-6 - When Things Fall Apart and CEO Psychology
Tony Hsieh, Delivering Happiness (2010), Ch. on Zappos culture and core values
Noam Wasserman, The Founder's Dilemmas (2012), Ch. on team formation and equity splits
First Round Review, 'The 30 Best Pieces of Advice for Startup Founders'

lecture_notes.introduction: Ben Horowitz, who co-founded Opsware (sold to HP for 1.65 billion) and now runs Andreessen Horowitz, wrote The Hard Thing About Hard Things to explain that the real challenges of building a startup are not strategic but human. 'The hard thing,' he writes, 'isn't setting a big, hairy, audacious goal. The hard thing is laying people off when you miss the big goal. The hard thing isn't hiring great people. The hard thing is when those great people develop a sense of entitlement and start demanding unreasonable things.' This framing captures something that most entrepreneurship courses get wrong: the technical and strategic challenges are solvable with enough thinking, but the people challenges test character in ways that cannot be prepared for academically. Tony Hsieh built Zappos into a billion-dollar shoe retailer by obsessing over culture from day one; he instituted the famous 'offer' where new hires could take 2,000 dollars to quit after training if they did not fit, on the theory that people who stayed would be committed. Zappos's culture was legendary until the 2020 sale to Amazon and Hsieh's tragic death showed the limits of any single leader's impact. On the opposite end, Uber's early culture of 'toe-stepping' and growth-at-all-costs under Travis Kalanick produced a brutal environment that eventually forced Kalanick's removal and cost the company an estimated 20 billion in goodwill. This session teaches you to hire intentionally, build culture deliberately, and navigate the personal challenges of leading teams through change.
key_concepts: Hiring priorities by stage: pre-seed (founders cover everything), seed (fill critical gaps not covered by founders), Series A (functional leaders in key areas), Series B+ (management depth and specialized expertise)
Founder gap analysis: list everything the business must do well, identify what each founder can do, what you can delegate, and what you must hire for; be honest about your gaps rather than defensive

Hiring bar: the quality threshold you require for every hire; a common mistake is lowering the bar when growth pressure is high, leading to cascading problems; great people attract great people, mediocre people attract more mediocre people

A-players only (Netflix culture memo): the Reed Hastings philosophy of only hiring top performers and paying them top-of-market to retain them; treating professional adults as adults rather than managing them with bureaucracy
Founders' Dilemmas (Wasserman): equity splits among co-founders, role clarity, vesting schedules with cliffs, CEO succession; getting these wrong early creates existential problems later
Cofounder vesting: standard is 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff; protects the company from early departures where one founder leaves with significant equity and derails everyone else
Culture as accumulated decisions: culture is not values on a poster; it is the sum of every hire, fire, promotion, meeting, and decision you make; intentional culture requires explicit choices

Culture deck (Netflix): the famous 127-slide document Hastings published in 2009 describing Netflix's expectations; articulated principles like 'freedom and responsibility' that shaped an entire generation of tech company cultures
Organizational transitions: specific pain points at 10, 50, and 150 employees; each transition requires new processes, new management layers, and changes in how the founders operate

Firing fast: the hardest discipline; Horowitz argues that the one thing founders universally regret is firing too slowly; when you know someone is not working out, the kind thing is to make the change quickly and treat them well in the transition
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Zappos's culture-first approach vs Uber's growth-first approach as contrasting case studies. Zappos under Tony Hsieh: 10 core values published and drilled, including 'Deliver WOW through service' and 'Embrace and drive change'; cultural fit was the primary hiring filter after baseline competence, and every candidate went through separate cultural interviews with no connection to technical skill assessment; the '2,000 dollar offer' let new hires quit after training if they did not fit, which filtered out people who came for the money rather than the mission; customer service agents had no scripts and no time limits, because Hsieh believed the best hiring filter was passionate people in empowered roles. Result: brand loyalty that drove organic growth, employee retention significantly above industry average, and a 1.2 billion dollar acquisition by Amazon in 2009 (Hsieh remained CEO). Uber under Travis Kalanick: 14 core values including 'toe-stepping' (asserting yourself aggressively), 'always be hustlin,' and 'principled confrontation'; aggressive performance reviews where bottom performers were actively managed out; famous culture issues including Susan Fowler's 2017 blog post about harassment and sexism that triggered an internal investigation and eventually forced Kalanick to step down; lost key leaders including Amit Singhal (fired for harassment history) and others; cost the company an estimated 20 billion in valuation and set up years of cultural reconstruction under Dara Khosrowshahi. Discuss how culture at both companies was not accidental; it was the direct result of leadership choices, hiring decisions, and what was rewarded and tolerated.
setup_requirements: Hiring scorecard template (Who by Smart and Street)
Founder equity split worksheet with vesting calculator
Sample culture decks from Netflix, HubSpot, and Valve
Access to Glassdoor for reviewing real company culture data

review.value: Hiring, firing, promoting, and managing performance cannot be delegated away in the early years; these decisions shape the company more than product or strategy decisions and require founder time and attention. Horowitz's book is required reading for anyone preparing for this responsibility

List what each founder is uniquely capable of, what can be delegated to mid-level hires, and what must be hired for at senior level; early hires should fill gaps not duplicate strengths. Many startups fail because founders hire people like themselves and leave critical skills uncovered

A culture document is only as strong as the daily decisions that reinforce it. Zappos and Netflix built cultures by making every hire, fire, and promotion consistent with their stated values. If you reward behavior that contradicts your values, the real culture follows the rewards

The most common regret among experienced founders is firing too slowly. When someone is not working out, the kind thing for them, the team, and the company is to make the change quickly. Treat departing employees with dignity, be honest about the reasons, and help them find their next role

class_schedule.topic: Scaling Challenges and Startup Failure Modes
learning_objectives: Identify common startup failure modes from CB Insights research including no market need, cash burn, wrong team, and failure to pivot
Analyze specific high-profile startup failures (Theranos, WeWork, Quibi, Jawbone, Solyndra) and extract the transferable lessons
Understand the governance and oversight failures that allow fraud and reckless capital consumption to continue unchecked
Apply scaling frameworks (Hoffman's Blitzscaling, Christensen's disruption model) to recognize inflection points
readings: Hoffman & Yeh, Blitzscaling (2018), Ch. 1-3 - What is Blitzscaling, Business Model Innovation
John Carreyrou, Bad Blood (2018), selected chapters on Theranos
CB Insights, 'Top Reasons Startups Fail' (annual report with 110+ failure postmortems)
Harvard Business School case study, 'WeWork and the Hype Machine'

lecture_notes.introduction: Most startups fail. That is not a pessimistic statement; it is a structural fact that every founder and investor must internalize. CB Insights's ongoing analysis of hundreds of startup failure postmortems finds that the top reasons are consistent: no market need (42 percent), ran out of cash (29 percent), wrong team (23 percent), got outcompeted (19 percent), pricing and cost issues (18 percent), and bad product (17 percent). Many failures involve multiple factors simultaneously. Some failures are spectacular: Theranos burned through 700 million dollars on a blood-testing technology that never worked, while Elizabeth Holmes committed fraud that eventually landed her in federal prison; WeWork raised over 10 billion dollars from SoftBank and others on a business model that lost money on nearly every building, then collapsed during its 2019 IPO attempt when the S-1 disclosures exposed governance failures, related-party transactions, and unsustainable economics; Quibi raised 1.75 billion for short-form mobile video content and shut down after six months with fewer users than the company had employees. These failures are valuable precisely because they are instructive: each one contains clear lessons that future founders can learn from, if they are willing to study failure rather than only studying success. Survivorship bias is one of the most destructive forces in entrepreneurship education; studying only winners produces false confidence about what drives success. This session systematically examines failure modes, works through high-profile failure case studies, and extracts principles that help future founders recognize and avoid the same traps.
key_concepts: No market need (CB Insights #1 failure cause): builders convinced themselves a product was needed without evidence; Quibi is the canonical recent example - no one had asked for premium short-form mobile video, and existing free alternatives (TikTok, YouTube) served the need adequately

Running out of cash: poor cash management, slow fundraising, or unexpected expenses that deplete runway before revenue catches up; often masked by optimistic projections that do not account for sales cycle length and churn

Fraud and governance failures: Theranos (fake technology demonstrations to investors and partners, falsified lab results), WeWork (Neumann's related-party transactions, use of WE trademark he personally owned, governance board unwilling to challenge him)

Founder conflict: disputes over equity, roles, strategic direction, or personal behavior that split the team; Jawbone's slow decline involved internal conflict and executive churn that compounded product missteps

Wrong team: technical team cannot ship, business team cannot sell, or founders cannot work together; Wasserman's Founder's Dilemmas documents how team composition predicts success better than most other factors

Failure to pivot: evidence that the current path is not working but leadership refuses to change; Solyndra burned 527 million dollars in federal loan guarantees on a cylindrical solar panel technology whose economics collapsed when conventional silicon panel prices dropped faster than expected

Blitzscaling (Hoffman): deliberately prioritizing speed over efficiency when you must capture a winner-take-all market before competitors; appropriate only for specific market structures and requires careful management because most of the errors of blitzscaling are fatal

Premature scaling (Startup Genome): scaling sales and marketing before product-market fit is validated; one of the most common failure modes because founders mistake early traction for genuine fit and pour capital into growth before the machine is proven

Zombie startups: companies that do not fail outright but cannot grow fast enough to justify continued investment or exit at a meaningful price; a quieter failure mode where the founders gradually realize the business is going nowhere

Post-mortem discipline: the most valuable exercise after any failure or setback is a blameless post-mortem that asks what happened, why, and what would we do differently; most failed startups skip this step and therefore do not learn
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work through the WeWork S-1 disaster of 2019 as a comprehensive case study in governance and economic failure. Setup: WeWork was founded in 2010 by Adam Neumann and Miguel McKelvey, raised early capital from Benchmark and others, then received massive investment from SoftBank's Vision Fund starting in 2017, peaking at a 47 billion dollar valuation before the IPO attempt. Red flags visible in the S-1: Neumann personally owned the WE trademark and had leased it to the company for 5.9 million dollars; Neumann personally owned several buildings that WeWork leased, creating direct conflicts of interest; the company was losing approximately 2 dollars for every 1 dollar of revenue; the governance structure gave Neumann super-voting shares and board control even at scale; the business model assumed long-term leases on the cost side and month-to-month subscriptions on the revenue side, creating an obvious duration mismatch; claimed technology platform was mostly PowerPoint branding rather than actual technology differentiation. Outcome: the S-1 filing was widely ridiculed, Benchmark and others forced Neumann's removal, the IPO was withdrawn, SoftBank took a multi-billion dollar writedown, and WeWork eventually went public at a fraction of the peak valuation before filing for Chapter 11 in 2023. Lessons: governance oversight failure allowed Neumann to operate without accountability; investors ignored unit economics because they wanted to believe the narrative; the business model never actually worked, regardless of the brand. Compare to Theranos: similar pattern of charismatic founder, weak governance, ignored unit economics (in Theranos's case, ignored scientific reality), and investor willingness to suspend disbelief.
setup_requirements: CB Insights failure postmortem database (free access)
Copy of Bad Blood (John Carreyrou's Theranos book) or the documentary
WeWork S-1 filing for direct analysis
Failure mode classification worksheet

review.value: Survivorship bias is the most destructive force in entrepreneurship education. For every Airbnb there are hundreds of failed hospitality startups; studying only winners produces false confidence about success drivers. Make a habit of studying failures in your target category

review.key: Governance Matters More Than It Seems
review.value: Weak governance structures (founder-dominant boards, super-voting shares, related-party transactions) enable the worst failures because no one is empowered to force change. Neumann's WeWork and Holmes's Theranos both happened because boards were unwilling or unable to push back

Running out of cash is the second most common startup killer and the most preventable. Track runway monthly, raise with margin, cut costs early when signs appear, and refuse to believe optimistic projections that put you outside cash-safety thresholds

Every setback, failed experiment, lost deal, or missed milestone is an opportunity for learning if you do a blameless post-mortem. The founders who learn fastest from failure are the ones who systematically examine what happened and update their models

class_schedule.topic: Exit Strategies (Acquisition, IPO, Secondary) and Course Synthesis
learning_objectives: Compare exit paths including acquisition, IPO, secondary sales, acqui-hire, and continuation via private markets
Understand the mechanics of acquisition processes including valuation methods, due diligence, earnouts, and post-acquisition integration
Evaluate the trade-offs founders face between early exits (liquidity, certainty) and holding out (optionality, compounding value)
Synthesize the full entrepreneurship journey from idea through exit and identify how each course topic connects to the others
readings: Feld & Mendelson, Venture Deals (5th ed.), Ch. 13-14 - Acquisitions and Going Public
Harvard Business Review, Josh Lerner, 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams' (excerpts on exits)
Bill Gurley, 'All Revenue Is Not Created Equal' and 'On the Road to Recap' essays
Sequoia Capital, 'Going Public' guide (sequoiacap.com)

lecture_notes.introduction: An exit is the event where founders, employees, and investors convert their ownership stakes into liquid value, and the decision about when and how to exit is one of the most consequential in a startup's life. The romanticized exit is the IPO, but only a small fraction of successful startups ever go public. Most successful exits happen through acquisition: Instagram sold to Facebook for 1 billion dollars just 18 months after founding and with only 13 employees; WhatsApp sold to Facebook for 19 billion dollars in 2014; GitHub sold to Microsoft for 7.5 billion in 2018. Others exit through secondary sales where founders and early investors sell shares to new investors while the company remains private; this has become increasingly common as companies stay private longer. A smaller subset reach IPO: Google went public in 2004 at a 23 billion dollar valuation, Facebook in 2012 at 104 billion, Airbnb in 2020 at 47 billion. Each path has different trade-offs in terms of certainty, control, dilution, and ongoing responsibilities. Some exits are not voluntary: acqui-hires where talent is the real asset, fire-sale acquisitions at distressed valuations, or wind-downs where remaining capital is returned to investors. Bezos and Amazon took 8 years and required substantial losses before going public in 1997 at a 438 million dollar valuation; the stock then rose by a factor of roughly 3,000 over the next 25 years, illustrating the enormous value left on the table for founders who sell too early relative to founders who compound value privately and publicly for decades. This session closes the course by mapping the full lifecycle from idea through exit and synthesizing how each previous topic connects to the decisions at the end.
key_concepts: Acquisition types: strategic (buyer values strategic fit, synergies, market position), financial (private equity buyer seeking returns through operational improvement), acqui-hire (buyer wants the team, not the product), distressed (forced sale at a discount)
Valuation methods for acquisitions: comparable transactions, DCF analysis, revenue multiples (common in SaaS: 5-15x ARR depending on growth rate), strategic premium above financial value
Due diligence: comprehensive review of financials, legal, technical, customer contracts, employees, and IP; can take 2-4 months and often uncovers issues that adjust price or kill the deal
Earnouts: portion of the purchase price contingent on future performance (revenue, milestones); common in smaller deals to bridge valuation gaps but often lead to disputes
Escrow and holdbacks: portion of purchase price held back for 12-24 months to cover potential claims against representations and warranties
IPO process: filing S-1 (extensive public disclosure), SEC review, roadshow to institutional investors, pricing, first-day trading; typically takes 6-12 months and costs tens of millions in fees
Direct listing (alternative to IPO): used by Spotify (2018) and Slack (2019) to go public without raising new capital or using underwriters; avoids lockup and pricing issues but loses the IPO pop

SPAC (Special Purpose Acquisition Company): shell company merges with a private target to take it public; boomed in 2020-2021 then collapsed; used by Virgin Galactic, Opendoor, SoFi with mixed results

Secondary sales: existing shareholders sell shares to new investors while company remains private; allows founder and early employee liquidity without an IPO; increasingly common as companies stay private longer

Exit timing trade-offs: early exit provides certainty and liquidity but forfeits future upside; Instagram sold for 1 billion in 2012, would plausibly be worth 50+ billion today; WhatsApp sold for 19 billion, would plausibly be worth more; Amazon and Google did not sell and created trillion-dollar outcomes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through two exit case studies side by side. Case 1: Instagram to Facebook, 2012. Founded 2010 by Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger after pivoting from Burbn (a location-based check-in app); raised 57 million across three rounds at a final private valuation of 500 million; sold to Facebook for 1 billion in cash and stock 18 months after founding with 13 employees and zero revenue. Decision factors: Systrom and Krieger were concerned about Facebook competitive response (Facebook was launching its own camera app), wanted the resources and distribution Facebook could provide, and saw the 1 billion as transformative personal wealth. Outcome: Systrom and Krieger stayed for six years, built Instagram into a 100+ billion dollar business unit inside Facebook, then left in 2018 over product disagreements with Mark Zuckerberg. Counterfactual: if Instagram had remained independent and IPO'd separately, it might be worth 200-500 billion as a standalone today. Case 2: Airbnb IPO, December 2020. Founded 2008, raised nearly 6 billion across multiple rounds, reached 47 billion valuation in 2019, collapsed to 18 billion in early 2020 due to COVID travel disruption, raised emergency debt financing, recovered in second half of 2020, IPO'd in December 2020 at 47 billion and popped to 100+ billion on first day. Decision factors: needed public liquidity for employees whose options were expiring, wanted to return capital to investors whose funds were near maturity, wanted to raise primary capital for growth. Outcome: successful IPO, Chesky and co-founders retained substantial ownership and control, company now worth roughly 90 billion. Key differences: Instagram's founders chose certainty and immediate wealth; Airbnb's founders chose optionality and long-term value creation. Both were rational responses to specific circumstances.
setup_requirements: Sample acquisition term sheet
Real S-1 filing (Airbnb or Snowflake) for structural analysis
Valuation comparison spreadsheet for public and private company multiples
Course synthesis review sheet connecting all 15 weeks

review.key: Exit Is a Choice, Not a Destination
review.value: Every exit path (acquisition, IPO, secondary, no exit) is a choice with trade-offs. Early exits provide certainty; holding out compounds value but risks everything. There is no universally right answer; it depends on the business, the market, and the founders' personal goals

Strategic buyers (Facebook for Instagram, Microsoft for GitHub) often pay dramatically more than financial buyers because they value synergies, competitive positioning, and talent. Know who your natural strategic buyers are from day one, because that shapes how you grow the company

Every topic in this course compounds: opportunity recognition informs customer discovery, which informs value proposition design, which drives MVPs, which generates traction, which supports fundraising, which funds scaling, which positions for exit. Skipping any step undermines everything that follows

review.key: Your First Venture Is Your Apprenticeship
review.value: Most first-time founders do not build Instagram or Airbnb; they build something smaller, learn enormous amounts from success and failure, and apply those lessons to their next venture or role. Treat your first venture as apprenticeship, not destination, and measure your progress by what you learned as much as what you built

quizzes.topic: Entrepreneurship Mindset, Opportunity Recognition, and Customer Discovery

questions.question: A graduate student approaches you with a startup idea: a mobile app that uses AI to recommend healthy restaurants based on user dietary preferences. He has been coding it for six months, has a polished prototype, and wants your advice on fundraising. When you ask about customer discovery, he says he has shown it to 'lots of friends and family who all love it.' Analyze the state of this opportunity using Blank's Customer Discovery framework, identify at least four specific problems with his approach, and recommend what he should do next before touching more code.
questions.answer:
State of the opportunity: this founder is in a classic pre-PMF trap. He has built before validating, he has treated friend-and-family feedback as signal (a Mom Test failure), he has spent six months of engineering effort based on unvalidated assumptions, and he has no evidence that anyone outside his social circle would pay for or even use the product.

Specific problems:

1. No get-out-of-the-building discipline: he has talked to friends and family, not real prospective customers who match a defined ICP (ideal customer profile). Friends and family will praise almost anything to protect the relationship, making their feedback nearly worthless as demand signal. The Mom Test rule: if they have not paid or taken a concrete commitment action, they are being polite, not honest.

2. Solution before problem validation: six months of coding means he validated the solution (he can build the app) but not the problem (do real people have a painful, urgent, frequent enough need for AI-driven healthy restaurant recommendations to change behavior and pay?). Most healthy-eating apps in this category have died because the need is mild and substitutes are free (Yelp filters, Google Maps).

3. No painkiller vs vitamin analysis: healthy restaurant recommendation is almost certainly a vitamin, not a painkiller. People who care deeply about healthy eating already have solutions (apps like HappyCow, meal delivery services, manual habits). The question is whether anyone experiences pain so acute they will adopt a new behavior to solve it.

4. No competitive or market size analysis: he has not identified direct competitors, indirect competitors (Yelp, Google Maps, HappyCow), or substitutes (asking friends, reading reviews manually); he has no bottom-up market sizing.

5. No willingness-to-pay evidence: the most important question - would a real stranger hand over money or commit concrete time for this? - has not been tested.

Recommended next steps:
- Stop coding immediately. Every hour of engineering at this stage makes the sunk-cost problem worse.
- Define an ICP in specific terms (e.g., '28-40-year-old professional women in NYC with dietary restrictions who eat out 4+ times per week').
- Conduct 20+ problem interviews with people matching the ICP (not friends); use Mom Test questions about past behavior, not hypothetical future behavior.
- Run a landing page test: describe the value proposition, offer a signup for early access, drive 500-1000 targeted visits via Meta Ads, measure conversion rate. If conversion is below 3 percent, the value proposition is not resonating.
- Attempt a concierge MVP: personally curate restaurant recommendations for 10 real users based on their preferences via text message, charge them 10 dollars for a month of recommendations. If you cannot get 3 of 10 to pay, there is no business.
- Write kill criteria in advance: 'If by week 8 I cannot get 20 completed interviews with ICP matches, or 3 paying concierge customers, I will sunset the project and move on.' Commit to this in writing.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the Mom Test failure and solution-before-problem pattern
Four or more specific problems with customer discovery approach
Concrete, actionable next steps including stopping code, interviews, landing page, and concierge MVP
Includes explicit kill criteria and painkiller vs vitamin reasoning

questions.question: Evaluate the following three venture opportunities on a 1-5 scorecard across four dimensions (founder-market fit, painkiller vs vitamin, willingness to pay evidence, defensibility). Rank them from most promising to least promising and explain your reasoning: (A) A former Stripe engineer building developer tools for API observability, targeting mid-size SaaS companies, with 3 paying design partners at 2,000 dollars/month already committed. (B) A first-year MBA student building a social app for pet owners to find playdates, with no prior experience in social apps or pets, validated by 150 survey responses saying 'I would use this.' (C) A former Whole Foods merchandising director building a B2B marketplace connecting small organic farms to restaurants, with a pilot deal signed with 4 restaurants and a full-time co-founder who was a regional sales manager at Sysco.
questions.answer:
Scoring:

Opportunity A - Developer tools for API observability:
- Founder-market fit: 5/5. Ex-Stripe engineer directly built and used observability tools at scale; deep domain expertise.
- Painkiller vs vitamin: 4/5. API observability is painful when things break; mid-size SaaS companies actively pay for tools like Datadog, Honeycomb, Observe. Strong painkiller but in a crowded category.
- WTP evidence: 5/5. Three paying design partners at 2,000/month is concrete revenue validation, not survey responses. Total 6,000 MRR with early evidence.
- Defensibility: 3/5. Developer tools category is competitive; defensibility would come from depth of integration, developer brand, and switching costs once embedded. Not winner-take-all yet.
Total: 17/20.

Opportunity B - Pet playdate social app:
- Founder-market fit: 1/5. First-year MBA with no experience in social apps, no experience with pets, no domain edge. Classic resume-driven startup idea.
- Painkiller vs vitamin: 1/5. Pet playdates are a mild social need at most; free alternatives (dog parks, existing pet communities) serve the need. No evidence this is painful enough to drive behavior change.
- WTP evidence: 1/5. 150 survey responses of 'I would use this' are worth almost nothing (Mom Test rule: stated intent is an optimistic lie). Zero paying users, zero commitments.
- Defensibility: 2/5. Would require network effects that are hard to bootstrap (chicken-egg problem in social apps is brutal); no clear moat.
Total: 5/20.

Opportunity C - B2B marketplace for organic farms and restaurants:
- Founder-market fit: 5/5. Ex-Whole Foods merchandising director knows farm supply; co-founder from Sysco knows restaurant distribution. Both sides of the marketplace covered by domain experts with networks.
- Painkiller vs vitamin: 4/5. Farm-to-restaurant supply is a real pain point; restaurants struggle with sourcing quality ingredients, farms struggle with stable demand. Strong painkiller.
- WTP evidence: 3/5. A signed pilot with 4 restaurants is concrete but early; not yet revenue-validated at scale.
- Defensibility: 4/5. Two-sided marketplaces build network effects once liquidity is achieved; founder networks on both sides create early-stage advantage.
Total: 16/20.

Ranking: A (17), C (16), B (5).

A and C are close and both fundable; the tiebreaker between them is traction and category. A has harder direct revenue evidence; C has stronger founder-market fit and a more defensible marketplace structure. B should not proceed without substantial revalidation of the fundamentals. The founder should either pivot to a category where she has founder-market fit or kill the idea.

rubric.key: Scores each opportunity across all four dimensions with reasoning
Correct recognition that A and C are strong and B is weak
Applies Mom Test principle correctly to survey data in opportunity B
Ranking with explicit reasoning about founder-market fit and WTP evidence

questions.question: Design a customer discovery interview script for a hypothetical startup building project management software specifically for small construction general contractors (GCs with 5-50 employees). Provide 8 specific questions following Mom Test principles, explain the purpose of each question, and identify at least three common anti-patterns the script avoids.
questions.answer:
Target: small construction GC owner or operations manager. Goal of interview: understand current project management workflow, identify painful workarounds, assess willingness to pay.

Interview script (8 questions):

1. 'Can you walk me through what a typical week looks like for you managing active projects? What were you working on yesterday and Monday?' Purpose: grounds the conversation in specific recent past behavior, not hypothetical future behavior. Reveals real workflow.

2. 'Of the projects you have running right now, which one is causing you the most headache, and what specifically is hard about it?' Purpose: identifies current pain points in concrete terms from a real project; the answer reveals whether pain is acute or minor.

3. 'When things go wrong on a project - a subcontractor misses a deadline or a spec changes - walk me through what happens. How do you find out? What do you do next?' Purpose: exposes the real information flow and crisis handling, not the idealized version. Reveals tools currently used (phone calls, texts, spreadsheets, existing software).

4. 'What tools or systems are you currently using to track progress? Can you show me how you use them?' Purpose: observes actual current behavior rather than asking hypothetically; if they show you a whiteboard, a paper binder, and texting, that is a clear signal.

5. 'Have you ever tried project management software? What was the experience like and why did you stop or keep using it?' Purpose: explores past attempts and churn reasons; construction is notorious for high software churn because products are not tailored to the workflow.

6. 'What do you pay for business software right now - accounting, payroll, anything? Can you estimate the total monthly?' Purpose: establishes baseline willingness to pay for software in general, which signals whether this buyer pays for anything or runs everything manually.

7. 'If a project falls behind schedule, what does that cost you in dollars - overtime, penalties, unhappy customers?' Purpose: quantifies the pain in economic terms, which informs pricing power and WTP.

8. 'Other than yourself, who else at your company would need to use a project management tool? What would they need it to do?' Purpose: identifies stakeholders and multi-user requirements; construction PM often needs mobile field access, which shapes product scope.

Anti-patterns avoided:

1. No pitching: the script never describes the product or asks 'would you use X?'. The founder learns from the customer's world before introducing any solution.

2. No hypothetical future questions: every question is anchored in specific past behavior ('yesterday,' 'current projects,' 'last time you tried'). This follows the Mom Test rule that stated intent is an optimistic lie.

3. No leading questions: questions are open-ended ('walk me through,' 'what is hard about it') rather than leading ('don't you think it would be great if...').

4. No vague abstraction: questions target specific situations and specific numbers (dollars per delayed project, exact tools, exact tasks) rather than generalities.

5. No founder ego: the script focuses entirely on the customer, not on validating the founder's preconceptions about what the product should be.

How to interpret results: if 15+ respondents mention similar pain points and current workarounds in their own words without being led, that is real pattern recognition. If responses are all different and scattered, the pain is not urgent or shared enough for a viable venture.

rubric.key: Eight questions grounded in past behavior, not hypothetical
Clear purpose identified for each question
At least three explicit anti-patterns avoided with reasoning
Guidance on interpreting results (pattern recognition threshold)

quizzes.topic: Business Model Canvas, Value Proposition, and Market Sizing

questions.question: Construct a Business Model Canvas (all 9 blocks) for Stripe at its 2011 launch, and then identify three ways the canvas has fundamentally evolved as of 2024. Explain why each change was necessary.
questions.answer:
Stripe 2011 Canvas:

1. Customer Segments: Developers at startups and small SaaS companies who needed to accept credit card payments online; frustrated with PayPal's redirect UX, Braintree's complexity, and the enterprise-focused merchant account process.

2. Value Propositions: Accept credit cards with 7 lines of code; signup in 5 minutes with just an email; transparent pricing at 2.9 percent plus 30 cents; developer-friendly documentation and API design.

3. Channels: Direct developer adoption through documentation quality, Hacker News and Twitter word of mouth, technical blog content, and self-service signup; no sales team.

4. Customer Relationships: Self-service signup and usage, email support with unusually fast response times, developer community through GitHub and Stack Overflow.

5. Revenue Streams: Processing fee per transaction (2.9 percent plus 30 cents); no monthly fees, no setup fees, no contracts.

6. Key Resources: Engineering talent (the Collison brothers plus early engineers), relationships with Wells Fargo and card networks, API infrastructure.

7. Key Activities: Infrastructure reliability, compliance (PCI, KYC, AML), fraud detection, developer relations, documentation quality.

8. Key Partnerships: Acquiring banks (originally Wells Fargo), card networks (Visa, Mastercard), compliance partners.

9. Cost Structure: Small team, infrastructure, card network fees (interchange pass-through), compliance, fraud losses.

Major changes by 2024:

1. Customer Segments expanded dramatically: from small developers and startups to large enterprises (Shopify, Amazon Marketplace, Uber, Instacart), marketplace platforms, global merchants in 50+ countries, subscription businesses. Stripe now sells to Fortune 500 companies through an enterprise sales team.

Why necessary: the startup segment alone could not support Stripe's valuation of 70+ billion dollars; scaling required moving upmarket to enterprises with larger transaction volumes and more revenue per customer. The small-developer segment remained important as a top-of-funnel pipeline but is no longer the primary revenue driver.

2. Value Propositions expanded from payments to a full financial stack: now includes Stripe Billing (subscriptions), Stripe Connect (marketplaces), Stripe Issuing (card issuing), Stripe Capital (business loans), Stripe Atlas (company formation), Stripe Tax (tax compliance), Stripe Identity (KYC), Stripe Treasury (banking-as-a-service). What started as credit card processing is now end-to-end financial infrastructure.

Why necessary: horizontal expansion captures more of the customer's total payments and finance spend (higher revenue per customer) and creates switching costs by embedding Stripe in more workflows, deepening the moat.

3. Channels expanded from self-service to include dedicated enterprise sales, solutions engineering, partner integrations, and developer relations programs; channels now include strategic partnerships with Shopify, Slack, and platform integrations.

Why necessary: enterprise customers do not sign up through a form; they require negotiated contracts, custom integrations, security reviews, and strategic relationships. Adding enterprise sales was essential for moving upmarket and capturing large customers who would never adopt self-service.

The core DNA (developer-first, infrastructure-grade reliability) remained constant, but the canvas evolved dramatically across segments, value propositions, channels, and revenue streams. Every other block adapted to support that evolution.

rubric.key: Complete accurate 2011 canvas with all 9 blocks
At least three substantive evolution changes identified
Correct reasoning for why each change was necessary (enterprise value, moats, etc.)

questions.question: A founder tells you her startup 'has a total addressable market of 500 billion dollars, the global e-commerce market, and we only need 0.5 percent to be a unicorn.' Explain in detail why this TAM claim is weak, and then rebuild a credible bottom-up market sizing for a hypothetical startup selling returns management SaaS to mid-market fashion e-commerce brands.
questions.answer:
Why the TAM claim is weak:

1. Wrong definition of market: the global e-commerce market is not the addressable market for any single product; it includes physical goods, services, platforms, marketplaces, B2B, B2C, regional variations, and thousands of subcategories. Returns management SaaS addresses a tiny slice of this number.

2. The 1 percent fallacy: no startup wins 0.5 percent of a 500 billion market by accident. Either you win a specific segment decisively (5-50 percent market share of a defined segment) or you do not win at all. 'We only need 0.5 percent' is a red flag that the founder has not thought carefully about how they will actually capture customers.

3. Top-down arithmetic is not defensible: no investor can pressure-test the number. What assumptions drove the 0.5 percent capture rate? What is the path from 0 to 0.5 percent? What does 0.5 percent look like in customers and ACV? The founder cannot answer, which signals unrigorous thinking.

4. Mismatch with go-to-market: even if the total were correct, the startup can only realistically reach a subset via specific channels and sales motions. The serviceable market is much smaller than the total market.

5. Investors pattern-match this language to weak pitches: the phrase 'global e-commerce market, we only need X percent' is a tell that the founder has not done bottom-up analysis. Experienced investors discount it severely.

Bottom-up rebuild for returns management SaaS for mid-market fashion e-commerce:

Step 1 - Define customer: mid-market fashion e-commerce brands with 10M to 500M in annual online revenue, for whom returns are a significant operational and financial pain. Exclude marketplace sellers (Amazon, eBay) who use marketplace returns tools, exclude the largest brands (Nike, H&M) who build internally, exclude brands under 10M in revenue (not yet sophisticated enough to buy software).

Step 2 - Count the customers: fashion e-commerce is dominated in the US by approximately 2,500 brands in the 10M-500M range, based on Forrester and eMarketer industry reports. Globally (including UK, EU, Australia, Canada), roughly 5,000 brands in this definition.

Step 3 - Estimate willingness to pay: research competitors (Loop Returns, Returnly, Happy Returns) and public filings; mid-market fashion brands typically pay 500-5,000 per month for returns management depending on volume, which is 6,000-60,000 ARR. Use 20,000 as the midpoint ACV.

Step 4 - Calculate TAM: 5,000 brands x 20,000 ACV = 100 million ARR TAM globally for this segment.

Step 5 - Calculate SAM: focus initially on the US and Canada where go-to-market is easier and competitive position is stronger. Addressable segment = 2,800 brands x 20,000 = 56 million ARR SAM.

Step 6 - Calculate SOM in 5 years: realistic market share against incumbent competitors (Loop, Returnly) is 10-15 percent, requiring 280-420 customers. 10 percent SOM = 5.6 million ARR.

Step 7 - Validate: cross-check against public information on Loop Returns (reported 100M+ in revenue by 2023, likely several thousand customers); this bottom-up estimate is consistent with real market data.

Result: 5.6M ARR SOM as a realistic 5-year target. This is smaller than '0.5 percent of 500 billion' but is defensible line-by-line and gives the founder a clear target: acquire 280 customers at 20K ACV. That is a concrete goal, not a fantasy.

rubric.key: Four or more specific reasons the TAM claim is weak
Rigorous bottom-up rebuild with segment definition, customer count, ACV, and multi-step calculation
Cross-check against real competitor data or industry reports

questions.question: Design a Value Proposition Canvas for Warby Parker at 2010 launch (customer side: jobs, pains, gains; value map side: pain relievers, gain creators, product/service) and explain specifically why the Home Try-On program was the pivotal element that made the business model work.
questions.answer:
Warby Parker 2010 Value Proposition Canvas:

Customer side - Jobs to be done:
- Functional: get prescription corrective eyewear that works for my vision and lifestyle
- Emotional: feel good about how I look in glasses, feel confident in my choice, reduce anxiety about a visible personal purchase
- Social: express personal style, be perceived as current and tasteful, not stuck with ugly glasses

Customer pains:
- Optical industry is overpriced: typical frames at LensCrafters marked up 10-20x from manufacturing cost because Luxottica owns most brands AND retail chains AND the insurance network; single pair of glasses typically 300-500 dollars
- Intimidating brick-and-mortar experience: pressure from salespeople, narrow selection under fluorescent lights, anxiety about choosing the right frames without unbiased input
- Time-consuming: multiple trips to stores, limited inventory, inability to evaluate frames in natural settings (home, work)
- Risk of buyer's remorse: hard to know how frames will look in real life after a short in-store evaluation
- Inconsistent returns policies: difficult to exchange or return eyewear that did not work out

Customer gains desired:
- Required: correct prescription, acceptable quality, reasonable price
- Expected: easy ordering process, convenient pickup or delivery, acceptable warranty
- Desired: style confidence, low price (100 dollars or less), ethical brand story, fast shipping
- Unexpected: ability to try on frames at home, share with friends and family for opinions, return anything easily

Value map side - Pain relievers:
- 95 dollar price point for prescription glasses (vs 400+ at incumbents)
- Home Try-On program: order 5 frames, try at home for 5 days, return in provided box with prepaid shipping
- Direct-to-consumer model bypasses optical retail markup
- Free returns and exchanges
- Buy one give one program (VisionSpring partnership) addresses ethical concern

Gain creators:
- Curated fashion-forward styles designed in-house rather than licensed from Luxottica brands
- Brand storytelling around young independent founders (Neil Blumenthal, David Gilboa, Andy Hunt, Jeffrey Raider, who met at Wharton)
- Simple website with clear photography and style information
- Lifestyle brand positioning that made glasses feel like a fashion accessory, not a medical device

Product/service:
- Prescription eyeglasses ordered online with prescription upload
- Home Try-On program (the hero feature)
- In-store experience added later
- Progressive lenses, sunglasses, contacts expanded over time

Why Home Try-On was pivotal:

1. It directly addressed the biggest pain point: anxiety about choosing frames without trying them on. Without this, Warby Parker would have been 'cheap glasses on the internet,' competing on price in a category where buyers cared deeply about fit and appearance.

2. It solved the attribution problem: customers who tried at home had already invested time and attention, making the eventual purchase decision much higher-commitment than an abandoned cart.

3. It created a social conversion event: trying on frames at home meant showing them to family, roommates, and friends, which generated word-of-mouth and brand visibility well beyond the individual customer.

4. It built a data flywheel: Warby Parker learned which frames people kept, which they returned, and why, enabling product decisions and recommendation improvements.

5. It was genuinely differentiated: no other eyewear retailer could offer this without completely restructuring their supply chain and returns logistics. LensCrafters could not replicate it because their business model depended on in-store traffic and sales pressure.

6. It converted skeptics: price alone was not enough for people to try an unknown online eyewear brand; the zero-risk nature of Home Try-On removed the last objection.

Result: Warby Parker grew from launch to 100 million in revenue in five years, reached a 3 billion dollar valuation by 2018, and IPO'd successfully in 2021 at 6 billion. The single product mechanic (Home Try-On) was what made the value proposition undeniable.

rubric.key: Complete customer side with jobs, pains, and gains in appropriate detail
Complete value map side with pain relievers, gain creators, and product/service
Strong explanation of why Home Try-On was the pivotal mechanic (at least 4 reasons)

quizzes.topic: Competitive Strategy, Lean Startup, and Unit Economics

questions.question: A SaaS founder shows you her unit economics: ACV is 12,000, gross margin is 75 percent, CAC is 8,000, blended monthly churn is 3 percent, and she has 200 customers generating 2.4M ARR. She raised 5M at a 25M post-money valuation and is burning 300K per month. Calculate LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, CAC payback period, and runway. Then diagnose whether this is a healthy business at its current stage and recommend specific actions.
questions.answer:
Calculations:

1. LTV: monthly ARPU is 12,000 / 12 = 1,000. Average customer lifetime = 1 / 0.03 = 33.3 months. LTV = 1,000 x 0.75 x 33.3 = 25,000.

2. LTV:CAC ratio: 25,000 / 8,000 = 3.1:1. This is at the 3:1 benchmark for healthy SaaS.

3. CAC payback: CAC / (monthly ARPU x gross margin) = 8,000 / (1,000 x 0.75) = 10.7 months. This is healthy (under 12 months).

4. Runway: assuming 5M raised minus some spend, and current burn of 300K/month, if she has about 4M cash remaining, runway is 13.3 months.

Diagnosis:

The unit economics are at or slightly above healthy benchmarks, but the overall picture has serious concerns at this stage:

Positive signals:
- LTV:CAC of 3.1:1 is at benchmark, suggesting the growth engine can be scaled without destroying unit economics.
- CAC payback of 10.7 months is healthy, meaning acquisition costs are recovered quickly enough to sustain growth capital.
- 2.4M ARR on 200 customers is solid early traction.
- 75 percent gross margin is healthy for SaaS.

Red flags:
- Monthly churn of 3 percent implies annual churn of roughly 31 percent (1 - 0.97^12 = 0.306), which is high for SaaS; typical B2B SaaS churn is 5-10 percent annually. This suggests either the target segment is wrong, the product has retention problems, or the onboarding is weak.
- If true long-term churn is worse than reported (common as cohorts mature), LTV will drop and unit economics will deteriorate quickly.
- Runway of 13.3 months is below the comfort zone (18+ months) for a SaaS company not yet at default-alive status.
- Burn of 300K/month against 200K monthly gross profit (2.4M ARR x 0.75 / 12) = net cash outflow of 100K/month from operations plus 200K of growth investment; this is a reasonable mix but tight.

Recommended actions:

1. Attack the churn problem first: a 3 percent monthly churn is the biggest leverage point. Conduct churn interviews with the last 20 cancelled customers (not asking 'why did you churn' but 'walk me through your usage over the last three months'); identify the root causes (onboarding, value realization, support, competitive loss, budget cut). If there are patterns, invest in onboarding, customer success, or product fixes that address them. Getting monthly churn from 3 percent to 1.5 percent would double LTV and transform the business.

2. Start fundraising planning now with 13 months of runway: do not wait. Begin conversations with potential Series B investors, prepare materials, and target closing within 4-6 months. The rule of thumb is to start fundraising with 12+ months of runway, not at the 6-month mark.

3. Test CAC reduction: are there channels with lower CAC? Specifically, invest in content marketing, SEO, and customer referrals to diversify the acquisition mix. Reducing CAC from 8,000 to 5,000 would lift LTV:CAC to 5:1 and dramatically improve the fundraising story.

4. Explore ACV expansion: what is the willingness of existing customers to pay more? Pricing research, upsell to higher tiers, or add-on modules could lift ACV from 12,000 to 18,000 without material acquisition cost, which would lift unit economics significantly.

5. Set cash discipline thresholds: track runway monthly, and if runway drops below 10 months without the new round closing, cut burn aggressively (reduce headcount growth, pause non-essential spend). Do not run toward zero on optimism.

Bottom line: the unit economics are survivable but not yet compelling for a strong Series B. The highest-leverage action is fixing churn, because it compounds through every other metric.

rubric.key: Correct calculations for LTV, LTV:CAC, payback, and runway
Recognition that monthly churn of 3 percent implies annual churn around 31 percent
Identification of runway pressure and need for immediate fundraising planning
Specific actionable recommendations prioritizing churn and fundraising

questions.question: A founder tells you: 'We have no competitors. This is a totally new category.' Explain why this statement is almost always wrong and dangerous, and walk through how you would construct a realistic competitive map for Slack (the workplace messaging product) as if you were doing the analysis in 2014 when Slack was first launching.
questions.answer:
Why 'no competitors' is wrong and dangerous:

1. Every customer already has some way of solving the problem. If your product exists to help teams communicate, teams were communicating somehow before your product (email, phone calls, meetings, in-person conversations). That is competition, even if it does not look like a competing product.

2. The status quo has zero switching cost. The customer's current solution (however bad) is easier for them to stick with than to evaluate and adopt yours. You are not just competing with other products; you are competing with inertia.

3. 'No competitors' signals unfamiliarity with the market. Experienced investors hear this and immediately worry that the founder has not done basic market research or does not understand that their product solves a problem someone else is already addressing, even imperfectly.

4. Genuinely new categories are extremely rare. Most innovation is reconfiguring existing solutions or serving underserved segments. Even products that created new categories (iPhone, Uber, Airbnb) all competed with existing alternatives (flip phones plus PDAs, taxis plus black cars, hotels plus Couchsurfing plus hostels).

5. Ignoring competitors leads to complacency. Founders who say they have no competitors are less likely to track competitive moves, anticipate disruption, or build defensibility, leaving them vulnerable when competition inevitably arrives.

Competitive map for Slack in 2014:

Direct competitors (similar products serving similar jobs):
- HipChat (Atlassian): team chat, existed since 2009, integrated with JIRA and Confluence; primary direct competitor at launch
- Campfire (37signals): established team chat product since 2006; smaller market share but loyal users
- Microsoft Lync / Skype for Business: enterprise chat and presence, huge enterprise installed base
- IRC: free, persistent chat protocol used by developer teams for decades
- Jabber / XMPP-based tools: older but still used by many organizations

Indirect competitors (different approach, same jobs):
- Email: the incumbent workplace communication tool, used by essentially every company; primary competitive threat because it is free, universal, and habitual
- Yammer (Microsoft): enterprise social network acquired by Microsoft in 2012, targeting the same 'better than email' message for enterprises
- Facebook at Work (later Workplace): rumored and eventually launched in 2015; major indirect threat from a company with strong product capabilities
- Google Hangouts: free, embedded in G Suite, minimal friction for Google customers
- Asana, Trello, Basecamp: project management tools that include communication features; overlap with chat use cases

Substitutes:
- In-person conversations (for co-located teams)
- Phone and conference calls
- Wiki tools and document collaboration (Google Docs, Confluence)
- SharePoint and enterprise intranets
- Doing nothing (accepting poor team communication as a fact of life)

Non-consumption:
- Small companies and teams that could not afford or justify enterprise communication tools; this was actually a large opportunity Slack exploited
- Developer teams using IRC that Slack could convert by offering a better-looking product with persistent history and integrations

Slack's competitive strategy in this landscape:

- Differentiation on polish and delight: much better UI than HipChat, Campfire, IRC, or Lync; made workplace chat feel like a consumer product
- Integration ecosystem as a moat: Slack invested heavily in third-party integrations (Google Drive, GitHub, Trello, thousands more) that made it the hub for workflow
- Freemium go-to-market: free for small teams, self-service signup, no sales call required; this bypassed enterprise procurement that protected incumbents
- Brand and voice: Slack's communication style was distinctive and became a marketing asset; customers loved the product experience
- Real target: replace email for internal team communication, which was a massive job-to-be-done that everyone complained about but no one had displaced

Outcome: Slack grew from launch in 2014 to 70+ million daily active users by 2019, IPO'd via direct listing in 2019 at 19 billion, and was acquired by Salesforce in 2021 for 27.7 billion dollars. The competitive map was crowded, and Slack's insight was that incumbents had all underinvested in product quality, integrations, and the specific job of replacing workplace email.

rubric.key: Five or more specific reasons 'no competitors' is wrong and dangerous
Complete competitive map with direct, indirect, substitutes, and non-consumption
Slack's differentiation strategy articulated with concrete elements

questions.question: Design a Lean Startup MVP plan for a hypothetical B2B startup targeting HR leaders at mid-market companies with a product that uses AI to analyze employee engagement survey responses. The founder has domain expertise from previous HR tech roles but has not written any code. Describe the sequence of experiments, what each experiment tests, cost, and success criteria before any production code is written.
questions.answer:
The riskiest hypotheses for this venture, in order: (1) Do HR leaders at mid-market companies actually experience enough pain from analyzing engagement survey results that they would pay for a solution? (2) Is AI-driven analysis valuable enough to HR leaders to pay for vs free alternatives (manual analysis, existing dashboards)? (3) Can the founder deliver results that HR leaders find useful?

Lean Startup MVP sequence:

Experiment 1 - Problem interviews (Weeks 1-4):
- Target: 25 HR leaders at US mid-market companies (500-5,000 employees)
- Activity: 30-minute problem interviews following Mom Test principles; focus on past behavior when analyzing engagement survey data, current workarounds, time spent, frustrations
- Cost: founder time only, roughly 60 hours
- Success criteria: at least 15 of 25 interviews describe real pain (time spent, inability to draw actionable insights, frustration with existing tools) in concrete terms without being led; at least 10 express interest in a solution that would help them analyze results faster or deeper
- Learning goal: validate whether this is a real problem worth solving

Experiment 2 - Landing page test (Weeks 5-6):
- Activity: build a simple Unbounce or Carrd landing page with a clear value proposition ('AI-powered analysis of employee engagement survey data - find actionable insights in minutes, not days'), a waitlist signup, and a Calendly link for 'schedule a demo' conversation
- Traffic: 1,000 targeted visits via LinkedIn Ads to HR leaders at mid-market companies, budget 1,500 dollars total
- Cost: approximately 2,000 dollars including tool setup
- Success criteria: conversion rate from visits to email signups above 4 percent (40 signups); at least 5 demo requests booked from the Calendly link
- Learning goal: validate whether the value proposition resonates beyond self-selected interview subjects

Experiment 3 - Concierge MVP (Weeks 7-12):
- Activity: recruit 3-5 HR leaders from the landing page signups who are willing to pay 500-1,000 dollars for a one-month analysis engagement; use manual analysis with existing AI tools (GPT-4, Claude, Python libraries) to analyze their engagement survey data; deliver a report with insights and recommendations
- Cost: approximately 500 dollars in API costs and tools, plus founder time
- Success criteria: at least 3 customers pay the concierge fee; 2 of 3 express interest in continuing the relationship; founder validates that meaningful insights can be extracted using AI from real data; founder documents the workflow that would need to be automated
- Learning goal: validate willingness to pay and feasibility of delivering value; identify which analysis techniques produce the most customer delight

Experiment 4 - Wizard of Oz MVP (Weeks 13-18):
- Activity: build a minimal web interface where HR leaders can upload a CSV of survey data and receive a PDF report within 24 hours; behind the scenes, the founder (or a contractor) runs the same analysis manually but faster
- Cost: 3,000-5,000 dollars (developer contractor to build upload interface, hosting)
- Success criteria: 10 paying customers at 500-1,000 dollars per month; Net Promoter Score above 40; customers request additional features that could be productized
- Learning goal: validate scalability of the workflow and refine the product vision before investing in automation

Experiment 5 - Sketched product roadmap (Weeks 19-24):
- Only after experiments 1-4 have produced positive signals does the founder consider building actual production software
- Use concierge and Wizard of Oz data to define exactly what needs to be automated, what customers valued most, and what features were asked for repeatedly
- Begin hiring a technical co-founder or contract engineer

Total cost of Experiments 1-4: approximately 5,500-8,000 dollars plus founder time. Total elapsed time: approximately 18 weeks. Total validated learning: whether the business exists, who the real customer is, what they will pay, and what the product must actually do.

Contrast this to the typical approach of spending 6-12 months and 100K+ on development before talking to customers. The Lean approach saves enormous amounts of money and time, and dramatically increases the probability of building a product that actually works.

Kill criteria in advance: if at week 4 fewer than 10 problem interviews confirm real pain, kill the venture. If at week 6 landing page conversion is below 2 percent, rework the value proposition or kill. If at week 12 fewer than 3 concierge customers pay, kill. Each stage has explicit decision criteria.

rubric.key: Sequence of at least 4 experiments with clear progression
Each experiment has hypothesis, activity, cost, and success criteria
Kill criteria and Wizard of Oz / concierge patterns applied correctly

quizzes.topic: Fundraising, Term Sheets, Dilution, and Pitch

questions.question: Walk through a cap table progression for a startup: two founders start with 50/50 equity and 10M shares total. They raise a 2M seed on a SAFE with a 10M post-money cap, then a 10M Series A at 40M post-money (pre-money 30M, with a 10 percent option pool expansion required before the round), then a 30M Series B at 120M post-money. Calculate founder ownership after each round and identify the total dilution. Then explain how liquidation preferences affect founder proceeds if the company sells for 200M at that point with 1x non-participating preferred.
questions.answer:
Cap table progression:

Starting point:
- Founder A: 5M shares (50 percent)
- Founder B: 5M shares (50 percent)
- Total: 10M shares, 100 percent founder ownership

Seed round (2M SAFE at 10M post-money cap):
When the SAFE converts at the Series A priced round, it converts at the lower of the cap or the Series A price. With a 10M post-money cap and 2M investment, the SAFE converts to 20 percent of the company on a post-money basis.

For simplicity, we can think of the conversion happening at the Series A: the SAFE holders take 20 percent of the company after conversion, diluting existing shareholders proportionally.

Before Series A math (post-SAFE conversion):
- SAFE holders: 20 percent
- Founders (combined): 80 percent
- Each founder: 40 percent

Pre-Series A option pool expansion:
Series A typically requires a 10 percent post-money option pool, created BEFORE the round (so it dilutes existing shareholders, not new investors). To end at 10 percent of post-money (40M valuation), pool needs to be about 12.5 percent of pre-money.

After option pool expansion (pre-Series A investment):
- Option pool: ~12.5 percent
- SAFE holders (diluted): ~17.5 percent
- Founders combined: ~70 percent (each at 35 percent)

Series A round (10M at 40M post-money):
New investors take 10M / 40M = 25 percent of the post-money company. Everyone else is diluted by 25 percent proportionally.

After Series A:
- Series A investors: 25 percent
- Option pool: ~9.4 percent
- SAFE holders (now converted to Series Seed equivalent shares): ~13.1 percent
- Founders combined: ~52.5 percent (each at 26.25 percent)

Series B round (30M at 120M post-money):
New investors take 30M / 120M = 25 percent of the post-money company. Everyone else is diluted by 25 percent proportionally.

After Series B:
- Series B investors: 25 percent
- Series A investors: ~18.75 percent
- Option pool: ~7 percent (may also be topped up, ignoring refresh for simplicity)
- SAFE holders: ~9.8 percent
- Founders combined: ~39.4 percent (each at roughly 19.7 percent)

Total founder dilution from start: from 100 percent to approximately 39.4 percent combined, or a 60.6 percent reduction in ownership percentage, over three rounds.

Exit analysis at 200M sale with 1x non-participating liquidation preference:

1x non-participating means each preferred series can either take their money back (liquidation preference) OR convert to common and share in proceeds pro-rata, whichever is greater.

Let's calculate what each investor gets on conversion vs on preference:

Series B: invested 30M; preference is 30M; conversion would get them 25 percent of 200M = 50M. Convert. Take 50M.

Series A: invested 10M; preference is 10M; conversion would get them 18.75 percent of 200M = 37.5M. Convert. Take 37.5M.

SAFE (Seed): effective investment roughly 2M (converted at the cap); preference is 2M; conversion would get them 9.8 percent of 200M = 19.6M. Convert. Take 19.6M.

Remaining for common shareholders (founders plus option pool): 200M - 50M - 37.5M - 19.6M = 92.9M

Founders combined (39.4 percent of common): but we need to recalculate once preferred convert. Since all preferred convert to common, the total share base is the full diluted count, and each shareholder takes their percentage of 200M.

Let me redo this: in a clean 1x non-participating scenario where everyone converts, each shareholder takes their percentage of the total proceeds.

Founders: 39.4 percent x 200M = 78.8M (combined), or about 39.4M each
Series B: 25 percent x 200M = 50M
Series A: 18.75 percent x 200M = 37.5M
SAFE holders: 9.8 percent x 200M = 19.6M
Option pool (distributed to employees): 7 percent x 200M = 14.1M

Total: 200M

The founders walk away with approximately 39.4M each, for a combined 78.8M. Not bad, but they gave up 60 percent of their ownership across three rounds to get there. Had they bootstrapped to the same 200M outcome, they would have walked with 100M each.

Key insights: each round dilutes, the option pool is a hidden dilution, and founders must weigh the value of capital (what it enables) against the cost (dilution). In this scenario, if the capital enabled the business to become a 200M exit vs a bootstrapped 50M exit, the dilution was worth it. If the business could have reached 100M bootstrapped, the dilution was a mistake.

rubric.key: Correct cap table progression through seed, Series A with pool, and Series B
Recognition that option pool refresh dilutes existing holders
Correct liquidation preference analysis (convert vs preference choice)
Insight about trade-off between dilution and business outcome

questions.question: Critique the following pitch deck opening and rewrite it to follow the Sequoia template and best practices: 'Slide 1: Our Company. Slide 2: Big Problem - people want healthier food but don't have time. Slide 3: Our Solution - an AI-powered meal planning and grocery delivery service. Slide 4: Our Team - we are passionate founders. Slide 5: Market Size - the US food market is 1.8 trillion dollars and we only need 0.1 percent.'
questions.answer:
Critique:

1. Slide 1 'Our Company' is empty: the first slide should state the company's core value proposition in one memorable sentence, not simply the name. Investors should leave the first slide knowing what you do.

2. Slide 2 'Big Problem' is vague: 'people want healthier food but don't have time' is generic enough to apply to dozens of existing products. It does not articulate the specific pain, who experiences it, or what they currently do about it. No concrete customer, no concrete numbers.

3. Slide 3 'Our Solution' mixes two products: meal planning and grocery delivery are very different businesses with different unit economics and competitors. Which is the actual product? What is the differentiation vs Instacart, HelloFresh, Mealime, and dozens of existing meal-planning apps?

4. Slide 4 'Our Team' says 'passionate founders' with no credentials: passion is assumed, not a differentiator. This slide should demonstrate founder-market fit with specific experience, degrees, prior companies, or domain expertise that makes the team uniquely qualified for this problem.

5. Slide 5 'Market Size' uses the 1 percent fallacy and a top-down TAM: 'only need 0.1 percent of 1.8 trillion' is exactly what investors mock. It signals no bottom-up analysis and no strategy for how to win specific customers.

6. Missing the Sequoia structure: no Why Now slide, no Competition slide, no Business Model slide, no Traction slide, no specific Ask. This is at most half a pitch deck.

Rewritten deck following Sequoia template:

Slide 1 - Company Purpose (one sentence):
'[Company Name] helps time-starved busy parents feed their families healthier meals without spending weekends meal planning or shopping.'

Slide 2 - Problem (concrete, specific):
'Two-career parents spend an average of 4 hours per week planning meals and shopping for their families, and still end up ordering takeout 3 nights a week out of exhaustion. We interviewed 47 parents; 41 described this as a chronic source of weekly stress. Existing solutions like HelloFresh are expensive and rigid; Instacart is transactional and does not help with planning; Mealime requires manual work.'

Slide 3 - Solution (specific, concrete):
'We are a weekly meal plan and grocery order service that automatically builds a healthy meal plan based on your family's preferences and dietary needs, orders the groceries through a partner retailer, and delivers recipes to your phone. One tap on Sunday evening, healthy meals all week. Starting at 39 dollars per week.'

Slide 4 - Why Now:
'Smartphone penetration, grocery delivery infrastructure (Instacart, Amazon Fresh, Walmart Grocery), and recent API access to grocery catalogs have made this possible at scale for the first time. Consumer willingness to pay for home meal services has doubled since 2019.'

Slide 5 - Market Size (bottom-up):
'Our target customer is US households with two working parents and children, approximately 15 million households. If we capture 5 percent over 5 years, that is 750K customers at 2,000 in annual revenue, or 1.5B ARR. SAM: 30B (target segment households x average spend). SOM (5-year): 1.5B ARR.'

Slide 6 - Competition:
'HelloFresh (expensive, rigid meal kits), Instacart (transactional only), Mealime (manual planning required), doing it yourself. We differentiate by combining AI-driven personalization, integration with existing grocery retailers, and minimal effort. Positioning chart shows us as uniquely combining automation, personalization, and cost.'

Slide 7 - Product:
'Screenshots of the weekly meal plan generation flow, grocery order placement, and recipe delivery app. Demo available.'

Slide 8 - Business Model:
'39 dollars per week subscription + 10 percent grocery partner commission. Unit economics: CAC 40 dollars, ACV 2,000, LTV 4,000 at current retention, payback 2 months.'

Slide 9 - Traction:
'200 paying customers in pilot, 15 percent MoM growth, 8 percent monthly churn, NPS 68. Early customer testimonials and cohort retention chart.'

Slide 10 - Team:
'Sarah Chen, CEO - former product lead at Instacart, 8 years in grocery tech. Mark Patel, CTO - former engineering manager at Uber Eats, built recommendation systems. Priya Johnson, COO - former VP Operations at Blue Apron. Advisors: former CPO of HelloFresh, former investor at Forerunner Ventures.'

Slide 11 - Financials and Ask:
'Raising 8M Series A to reach 2M ARR within 18 months. Use of funds: 4M engineering (expand product), 2M marketing (paid acquisition scaling), 1.5M operations (customer success and partnerships), 500K runway buffer. Lead investor: in discussions. 18 months runway to next round at 15M ARR target.'

Why this works better: every slide has specific numbers or claims, the story builds logically, the team slide demonstrates founder-market fit, the market size is bottom-up and defensible, competition is explicitly addressed, and the ask is concrete. A VC reading this deck understands what you do, why it matters, why now, who you are, and what you need from them. The original deck did none of that.

rubric.key: Five or more specific critiques of the original deck including TAM fallacy and vagueness
Rewritten deck covers at least 10 of the Sequoia template sections
Concrete specifics (numbers, names, bottom-up math) throughout the rewrite

questions.question: A founder is choosing between two term sheets for a Series A round. Term Sheet 1: 12M investment at 48M post-money, 1x non-participating preferred, weighted average anti-dilution, 10 percent option pool, standard protective provisions, one board seat.
