Term Sheet 2: 15M investment at 50M post-money, 1.5x participating preferred, full ratchet anti-dilution, 15 percent option pool, extensive protective provisions, two board seats and veto on CEO compensation. Which would you recommend and why?
questions.answer:
Recommendation: Term Sheet 1, despite the slightly lower valuation.

Analysis:

1. Headline valuation comparison:
Term Sheet 1: 12M / 48M = 25 percent dilution for the round
Term Sheet 2: 15M / 50M = 30 percent dilution for the round

So Term Sheet 2 has a higher pre-money (35M vs 36M, nearly identical) but takes more dilution because of the larger round size. The headline valuations are approximately equivalent; Term Sheet 2 provides 3M more capital in exchange for more dilution, which is reasonable in isolation.

2. Liquidation preference:
1x non-participating (Term Sheet 1): investors take their money back OR convert to common pro-rata, whichever is greater. At exit, they do not double-dip.
1.5x participating (Term Sheet 2): investors take 1.5x their money back (22.5M on 15M invested) AND then participate pro-rata in the remaining proceeds. This can dramatically reduce founder proceeds at exit.

Example: 100M exit
- Term Sheet 1: investors take max of 12M or 25 percent x 100M = 25M; they convert, take 25M; founders and employees split 75M.
- Term Sheet 2: investors take 22.5M preference first, then 30 percent x (100M - 22.5M) = 23.25M, totaling 45.75M; founders and employees split 54.25M.

The difference at a 100M exit is approximately 20M less for founders and employees under Term Sheet 2. The 'better' valuation has been more than erased by the worse terms.

3. Anti-dilution:
Weighted average (Term Sheet 1): standard, protects investors against down rounds by adjusting conversion ratio fractionally. Founder-friendly baseline.
Full ratchet (Term Sheet 2): extreme, adjusts investor conversion price to the new lowest price, which can be devastating if you ever need to raise a down round. Full ratchet is considered predatory and is rare in modern term sheets.

Example: if Series B is raised at a lower price per share than Series A, under full ratchet the Series A investors' conversion price resets to the Series B price, dramatically increasing their ownership and further diluting founders. This effectively makes any down round catastrophic for founders.

4. Option pool:
10 percent (Term Sheet 1): standard, comes out of pre-money founder equity
15 percent (Term Sheet 2): larger, more dilutive to founders, signals that investors expect more hiring than the founders may have planned

5. Protective provisions:
Standard (Term Sheet 1): typical veto rights on selling the company, raising debt, changing the cap table, approving budget
Extensive (Term Sheet 2): likely includes veto on CEO compensation, hiring senior executives, strategic decisions; this substantially reduces founder control and operational flexibility

6. Board composition:
One board seat (Term Sheet 1): founders likely retain majority or even control; typical 5-seat board with 2 founders, 1 investor, 2 independents
Two board seats plus comp veto (Term Sheet 2): investors have much more influence; comp veto is an unusual and intrusive term that would let investors block founder raises and bonuses

7. Overall judgment:
Term Sheet 2 offers 3M more capital in exchange for terms that could cost the founders 10-20M+ at exit and significant control over the operating business. The additional 3M is not worth the cost.

Term Sheet 1 is a standard, clean, founder-friendly Series A deal that would be considered reasonable by experienced founders and advisors. Accept Term Sheet 1.

If the founders feel they need the extra 3M of capital, they should go back to Term Sheet 1's investor and ask for additional capital at the same terms, or find another investor to fill the round. Accepting bad terms for marginally more capital is one of the most common and most expensive mistakes first-time founders make.

Reference: Brad Feld and Jason Mendelson's Venture Deals walks through exactly this analysis and is the standard guide for founders evaluating term sheets.

rubric.key: Correct recommendation of Term Sheet 1 with clear reasoning
Concrete comparison of liquidation preferences with example math
Identification of full ratchet as predatory and weighted average as standard
Recognition of the option pool and protective provisions as additional concerns

quizzes.topic: Scaling, Failure Modes, and Exit Strategies

questions.question: Apply the CB Insights failure mode framework (no market need, cash burnout, wrong team, got outcompeted, pricing/cost issues, bad product) to analyze the failure of Quibi. Which failure modes were most responsible, what specifically went wrong at each step, and what could have been done differently?
questions.answer:
Quibi failure analysis:

Background: Quibi was founded in 2018 by Jeffrey Katzenberg (former DreamWorks, Disney) with Meg Whitman (former eBay, HP) as CEO, raised 1.75 billion dollars in pre-launch funding from Hollywood studios, tech investors, and strategic partners, launched in April 2020 with a premium short-form mobile video subscription at 5-8 dollars per month, and shut down in December 2020.

Failure mode analysis:

1. No market need (primary cause, by a large margin):
The fundamental error was building a product for a market that did not exist. Quibi's core thesis - that consumers would pay for high-production-value short-form mobile video - was never validated with real users. The team assumed that Hollywood production quality plus mobile-first format would command a premium subscription. In reality: (a) consumers had TikTok and YouTube Shorts for free short-form content, (b) consumers used Netflix and Disney Plus for premium long-form content on their phones, tablets, and TVs, and (c) no one was asking for a premium short-form subscription. The category itself was imaginary.

What could have been done: customer discovery with actual target users before raising 1.75 billion dollars; a minimum viable product launched at a tiny scale to test demand; a willingness-to-pay test with a landing page and pre-orders; basically any of the Lean Startup practices that any 22-year-old founder would apply. Quibi's leadership came from the old Hollywood model where consumer research meant focus groups about existing content, not testing whether a new consumer behavior would emerge.

2. Got outcompeted (secondary, but closely related):
TikTok was growing explosively during Quibi's development; by launch, TikTok had hundreds of millions of users, was free, and offered short-form video with far superior distribution (algorithmic feed, social features, creator community). YouTube Shorts launched in September 2020 inside Quibi's launch window and was also free. Quibi's premium pricing could not compete against free alternatives that were clearly good enough for the use case.

What could have been done: competitive analysis that treated free alternatives as real competitors (not just other premium services) would have exposed the pricing problem. The team appeared to dismiss TikTok and YouTube as 'user-generated' and therefore not comparable, which was a catastrophic misreading of consumer behavior.

3. Pricing and cost issues (significant contributor):
Quibi spent enormous amounts on original content from Hollywood talent (Steven Spielberg, Chrissy Teigen, LeBron James-produced shows) and on celebrity marketing. Content costs alone were hundreds of millions. At 5-8 dollars per month per subscriber, Quibi needed to reach 20-40 million subscribers to cover its content budget, which was unrealistic from launch. The business model was structurally broken from day one: content costs were set by Hollywood expectations, but consumer willingness to pay was set by Netflix, which had 200+ million subscribers to spread content costs over.

What could have been done: a much smaller content library at launch, user-generated or lower-budget content to test the behavior before committing to Hollywood production, or a free ad-supported tier to reach scale before charging.

4. Bad product (contributor):
Significant product issues: the initial product did not support casting to TVs (a deliberate choice to enforce 'mobile-first' usage), did not support screenshots or screen recording (protecting exclusivity but preventing social sharing), launched during COVID-19 when mobile-first video consumption was actually lower than pre-COVID because people were stuck at home and preferred watching TV. These were solvable product issues, but the underlying market demand was the real problem.

What could have been done: minimum ship with TV casting; social features for sharing; but fundamentally, better product would not have fixed no market need.

5. Wrong team (secondary contributor):
The founding leadership was deeply experienced in traditional Hollywood entertainment, not consumer internet products or subscription services. Katzenberg was a visionary studio executive; Whitman was a seasoned Fortune 500 CEO; neither had built a consumer subscription product or a mobile-first service for millennial/Gen-Z audiences. They were not the team to figure out whether this category existed, and they surrounded themselves with people who shared their worldview.

What could have been done: hiring technical and consumer internet leadership that had built successful subscription services or mobile-first consumer products before committing the capital.

6. Cash burnout (consequence, not cause):
The 1.75 billion was spent on content, technology, and marketing without generating corresponding revenue. Burn rate at full scale was unsustainable against actual subscriber numbers (Quibi never exceeded roughly 1.5 million paid users, far below the roughly 7 million needed at 5 dollars per month to approach content cost recovery). Cash ran out because revenue never came close to expectations.

Primary failure: no market need. Every other issue was either a consequence of that or amplified by it. The lesson is that no amount of money, celebrity, or experience can rescue a venture if the fundamental market hypothesis is wrong.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies no market need as primary failure
Secondary failures identified with specific evidence (TikTok, pricing, product)
Concrete 'what could have been done' recommendations grounded in Lean Startup
Recognition that cash burnout was consequence not cause

questions.question: Compare the exit decisions of Instagram (sold to Facebook for 1 billion in 2012) and Airbnb (IPO in 2020 at 47 billion). Analyze the trade-offs, factors influencing each decision, and what transferable lessons founders should take away when considering their own exit options.
questions.answer:
Instagram exit to Facebook (April 2012):

Context: Instagram was founded in October 2010 by Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger after pivoting from Burbn (a location-based check-in app). By early 2012, Instagram had 30+ million users and 13 employees, zero revenue, and had just closed a 50 million Series B at 500 million post-money valuation. Facebook acquired Instagram for 1 billion in cash and stock two weeks before Facebook's own IPO.

Decision factors:

1. Competitive pressure: Facebook was preparing its own photo-sharing app and had the resources, distribution (900 million users), and relationships to compete directly. Systrom and Krieger feared a direct competitive response from a much larger company.

2. Resource access: Facebook offered infrastructure, engineering talent, and distribution that Instagram could not easily replicate as a standalone company.

3. Personal wealth: 1 billion dollars transformed Systrom and Krieger's personal financial circumstances permanently. They went from well-funded entrepreneurs to highly wealthy individuals overnight.

4. Autonomy promise: Facebook committed (at least initially) to let Instagram operate independently, which eased concerns about losing the product vision.

5. Speed: the deal closed in about 48 hours of negotiation, driven by Zuckerberg's personal push; Systrom and Krieger chose certainty over optionality.

Outcome: Systrom and Krieger stayed for six years, built Instagram into a 1 billion+ user platform generating tens of billions in revenue inside Facebook, then departed in 2018 over product disagreements with Zuckerberg. Instagram became arguably Facebook's most valuable asset. Standalone, Instagram would plausibly be worth 300-500 billion today.

Counterfactual: if Instagram had stayed independent, it would likely have become a major standalone platform, but it also would have faced the existential risk of Facebook's competitive response at a vulnerable growth stage. Systrom in interviews has said he does not regret the decision even knowing the eventual valuation, because the risk of Facebook crushing them was real.

Airbnb IPO (December 2020):

Context: Airbnb was founded in 2008, raised nearly 6 billion across multiple rounds, reached a 47 billion private valuation in 2019 funded by SoftBank and others. When COVID-19 hit in March 2020, bookings dropped 72 percent in weeks, the company laid off 1,900 employees (25 percent of staff), raised emergency debt financing at unfavorable terms, and wrote down its internal valuation to 18 billion. In the second half of 2020, travel recovered faster than expected (especially domestic and rural travel, which played to Airbnb's strengths vs hotels), and the company rapidly prepared for a December 2020 IPO, ultimately pricing at 47 billion and trading up to 100+ billion on the first day.

Decision factors:

1. Employee liquidity: many employees had options expiring or vesting that required a liquidity event; the IPO provided that path without being acquired.

2. Investor return timing: several early investors had funds nearing maturity and needed exit paths for their LPs; the IPO let them distribute shares or sell into the public market.

3. Primary capital: the IPO raised new capital that strengthened the balance sheet after the COVID shock.

4. Independence preserved: Chesky and co-founders retained super-voting shares that preserved their control of the company post-IPO, unlike an acquisition where they would have lost it.

5. Market timing: public markets in late 2020 were unusually receptive to travel recovery stories, allowing Airbnb to price at a higher valuation than private markets would have offered.

Outcome: successful IPO, Chesky remains CEO, Airbnb operates independently and continues to grow as a public company worth roughly 90 billion as of early 2026. Early investors achieved strong returns, employees received significant liquidity, and the company retained strategic flexibility.

Transferable lessons:

1. Exit is a choice with real trade-offs: Instagram chose certainty and immediate wealth; Airbnb chose optionality and long-term value creation. Neither is universally right; both were rational responses to specific circumstances.

2. Competitive pressure can force exit: Instagram's decision was heavily influenced by Facebook's imminent competitive response, which is a common reason for acquisition at early stages even when founders would prefer to continue.

3. Super-voting shares preserve founder control post-IPO: Airbnb used dual-class stock to ensure Chesky could continue running the company the way he wanted; Google, Facebook, and many other founder-led companies used similar structures. If founders want long-term control, dual-class structures are almost essential.

4. Personal wealth impact of exit is real: 1 billion to two founders (approximately 400 million each after investor dilution) was genuinely transformative for Systrom and Krieger. Founders should not underestimate the impact of certainty on their personal lives; optionality is not always the right choice for someone who has never had financial security.

5. Exit timing interacts with market conditions: Airbnb benefited from unusually favorable public market timing; different timing could have meant a very different valuation. Market conditions are not controllable, so exit timing is partly opportunistic.

6. Acquisition vs IPO is not the only choice: secondary sales (existing shareholders selling to new investors while the company stays private) have become increasingly common and provide liquidity without requiring a full exit decision.

7. Know your natural strategic buyers from day one: who would want to own your company and why? Instagram knew Facebook was a natural strategic buyer; this shapes how you grow the business and who you talk to along the way.

8. Founders of acquired companies often leave within 3-5 years: Systrom and Krieger left Instagram after six years over product disagreements; this is a common pattern. If long-term operational control matters to you, acquisition may not be the right path even if the financial outcome is strong.

rubric.key: Correctly contrasts the decisions and outcomes with factual detail
Identifies 5+ decision factors for each exit with reasoning
Extracts at least 6 transferable lessons applicable to other founders

questions.question: A startup founder with a profitable, slow-growing software business (2M ARR, 20 percent YoY growth, bootstrapped, 10 employees) is being pressured by an angel investor to raise venture capital and blitzscale the company. The founder is debating whether to raise a Series A or continue bootstrapping. Analyze the decision using the frameworks from the course and recommend a path.
questions.answer:
Decision analysis:

Current state: 2M ARR, 20 percent YoY growth (healthy but not venture-scale), bootstrapped, 10 employees, profitable (implied). This is a classic lifestyle/small business trajectory that has proven product-market fit but is not in winner-take-all mode.

Central question: is this business a good fit for venture capital? Remember from Week 10 that VC is appropriate for businesses that need to reach massive scale quickly in winner-take-all markets; it is inappropriate for profitable niche businesses where founders want control and optionality.

Arguments for raising VC:

1. Accelerate growth: 20 percent YoY growth is below VC expectations (VC benchmarks are 100+ percent at this stage); capital could fund sales, marketing, and product investment that accelerates growth substantially.

2. Market opportunity: if the market is genuinely large and winner-take-all dynamics exist, moving slowly could allow a competitor to capture the market first.

3. Optionality: raising capital buys optionality - the ability to pursue larger markets, bigger bets, and international expansion that bootstrapped growth cannot fund.

4. Angel investor's perspective: angels often push for VC rounds because it provides them an exit path and validates their early investment; this aligns their interests with fundraising, not necessarily with the founder's interests.

Arguments for continuing to bootstrap:

1. Business model fit: profitable bootstrapped SaaS at 2M ARR with 20 percent YoY growth suggests the business naturally compounds without needing growth capital. Mailchimp, Basecamp, and GitHub all followed similar trajectories successfully.

2. Control: raising VC means accepting board seats, protective provisions, option pool dilution, and pressure toward aggressive growth. Founders typically go from 100 percent ownership to 30-50 percent combined by Series B, and lose majority control of the board.

3. Growth expectations mismatch: 20 percent YoY is not a VC-scale growth rate. Investors who write checks at this stage will push for growth rates of 100+ percent within 12 months, which requires dramatic changes to hiring, spending, and risk-taking. If the market does not support that growth, the capital becomes a burden not an asset.

4. Exit optionality: bootstrapped companies have more exit options. A 20M acquisition makes the founders wealthy; under VC, the same 20M acquisition might leave the founders with very little after preferences, and the investors might block the sale in favor of pushing for a bigger exit that may never come.

5. VC math requires fund returners: at 2M ARR with 20 percent growth, even achieving 20M ARR in 3-4 years (10x) is unusual. At typical VC multiples, that might be 200M in enterprise value, which is a solid outcome but not a 'fund returner' for a 500M VC fund that needs 10+ billion outcomes to succeed. The math may not work for either side.

6. Founder life quality: founders of VC-backed companies typically work 70+ hours per week under intense pressure for years; bootstrapped founders have more control over their lifestyle and typically report higher satisfaction.

Diagnostic questions the founder should answer:

1. What is the realistic ceiling on this market? Could the business genuinely reach 100M+ ARR with enough capital, or is the natural ceiling 20-30M?

2. Are there competitors raising venture capital and growing faster? If so, competition may force the decision.

3. What would the founder actually do with 10M of capital? Specifically: hire which roles, spend on which channels, achieve which milestones? If the answer is vague, the business does not need the capital yet.

4. What is the founder's personal preference? Does she want to run the company independently for 10+ more years, or does she want a fast exit in 3-5 years? VC optimizes for the latter, bootstrapping for the former.

5. What exit outcome would the founder consider successful? A 50M acquisition that leaves her with 30M+ after preferences under bootstrapping? Or a 500M IPO that might produce 50M after preferences and control loss under VC?

Recommendation: Continue bootstrapping, with conditions.

The business profile suggests natural bootstrap fit: profitable, healthy growth, no evidence of winner-take-all dynamics forcing speed. The angel investor's pressure should not drive the decision; angels have different incentives than founders.

However, the founder should:

1. Build a simple 'what would change with capital' plan to test whether the business could absorb and deploy 5-10M productively. If the plan is concrete and defensible, the business may actually be ready for a small growth round later.

2. Consider alternatives to traditional VC: revenue-based financing (Pipe, Lighter Capital, Clearco) provides growth capital without equity dilution and is well-suited to profitable SaaS. Credit facilities can fund specific initiatives without selling equity.

3. Watch competitors carefully: if a venture-funded competitor emerges and starts capturing market share rapidly, the calculation could change. Keep optionality.

4. If raising, do it from a position of strength: with 2M ARR and profitability, the founder has leverage. Any round should be at a strong valuation with founder-friendly terms (1x non-participating, weighted average anti-dilution, one board seat, standard protective provisions). Never raise out of desperation.

5. Read Venture Deals and the Mailchimp, Basecamp, and GitHub stories. Bootstrapped founders have built companies worth billions without VC; the path is proven.

Bottom line: do not let someone else's ambition or incentives force a decision that may not fit your business or your life. Bootstrap unless there is clear evidence that capital is necessary and the business model fits winner-take-all dynamics.

rubric.key: Applies correct framework (match capital to business model) explicitly
Considers both sides of the argument with 4+ specific points each
Concrete recommendation with conditions and alternatives
References to course material (Mailchimp, bootstrapping, VC math) showing integration

major_assignments.title: Customer Discovery and Opportunity Validation Report
major_assignments.description: Select a venture opportunity (your own idea or an assigned case) and conduct a rigorous customer discovery process using Blank's Customer Development methodology. Interview at least 20 real prospective customers matching a defined ICP, synthesize the findings into pattern-recognition insights, identify whether the problem is validated as painkiller vs vitamin, and produce a formal report with recommendations. The goal is to practice real customer discovery under realistic constraints, not simulate it.
objectives: Apply Mom Test interview principles to extract honest customer feedback
Synthesize interview data into pattern-recognition insights beyond anecdote
Produce a Value Proposition Canvas grounded in real customer evidence
Make a clear go/pivot/kill recommendation backed by data

major_assignments.title: Business Model Canvas, Financial Model, and Unit Economics Analysis
major_assignments.description: For your chosen venture (same as Assignment 1, refined by discovery findings), build a complete Business Model Canvas, construct a bottom-up financial model with unit economics, calculate CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, payback period, and burn rate, and produce a market sizing analysis (TAM, SAM, SOM) using bottom-up methodology. This assignment forces rigorous thinking about the economic viability of the business, not just the customer and product.
objectives: Construct a coherent business model across all 9 canvas blocks
Build a defensible bottom-up financial model with explicit assumptions
Calculate and interpret unit economics metrics for the chosen business model
Produce a credible market sizing that would withstand investor scrutiny

major_assignments.description: Produce a complete investor pitch deck for your chosen venture and deliver a 15-minute live pitch to a panel of faculty and invited guest investors, followed by 10 minutes of Q&A. course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all class sessions. Workshops, guest founder sessions, peer pitch practice, and in-class discussions cannot be replicated after the fact. More than three unexcused absences may reduce the participation grade. Illness and documented emergencies are excused with advance notice when possible.
course_policies.late_submissions: Major assignments lose 10 percent per day late for up to 5 days, after which they will not be accepted. The final pitch cannot be rescheduled except for documented emergencies. Customer discovery interview deadlines are firm because the assignment depends on sequential iteration.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussing customer discovery methodology, brainstorming ideas, and peer-reviewing pitches with classmates is encouraged. All submitted written work, financial models, and pitch materials must be the student's individual work. Interview data must be original and collected directly by the student; copying or fabricating interviews constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. AI tools such as ChatGPT and Claude may be used for brainstorming, drafting, and financial model debugging, but final work must represent your own analysis and writing with AI use disclosed. Fabricating customer interviews, financial data, or market research constitutes academic dishonesty and will result in a failing grade for the course and referral to the academic integrity office.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop, Google Workspace or Microsoft Office access, Crunchbase free tier for market research, Strategyzer free tier or printed Business Model Canvas templates, Canva or Figma for pitch deck design, access to a recording tool for customer interviews (with interviewee consent), and willingness to reach real prospective customers outside their personal network. Guest founder sessions and pitch panels will be held in person or via Zoom, so a stable internet connection and webcam are required.
